  modelName: string,
  baseUrl: string,
  resourceGroup: string,
): Promise<string> {
  const response = await fetch(`${baseUrl}/lm/deployments?$top=100&status=RUNNING`, {
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
      "AI-Resource-Group": resourceGroup,
    },
  });

  if (!response.ok) {
    const error = await response.text();
    throw new Error(`Failed to list deployments (${response.status}): ${error}`);
  }

  const data = (await response.json()) as {
    resources: Array<{
      id: string;
      status: string;
      details?: { resources?: { backendDetails?: { model?: { name?: string } } } };
    }>;
  };

  const deployments = data.resources
    .filter((r) => r.status === "RUNNING")
    .map((r) => ({ id: r.id, modelName: r.details?.resources?.backendDetails?.model?.name ?? "" }))
    .filter((d) => d.modelName);

  const found = deployments.find((d) => d.modelName === modelName);
  if (!found) {
    const available = deployments.map((d) => d.modelName).join(", ");
    throw new Error(
      `Model '${modelName}' has no running deployment in your SAP AI Core tenant.\n` +
      `Available models: ${available}\n` +
      `Update AICORE_MODEL in your .env to one of the available models.`
    );
  }

  return found.id;
}

What this file does:

  • createSapAiCoreProvider() - validates credentials, fetches an OAuth token, discovers the deployment for your configured model, and returns a factory that registers it with Pi. If the model has no deployment, throws a clear error listing what IS available.
  • fetchAccessToken() - gets an OAuth token using client credentials (same auth the Python SDK does internally)
  • resolveDeploymentId() - lists running deployments and finds the one matching your model name. Deployment IDs are dynamic (they change on re-deploy), so we resolve them at startup rather than hardcoding.

The key insight: SAP AI Core's Azure-backed models (GPT-4o, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini) expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at /inference/deployments/{id}/v1/chat/completions. By setting api: "openai-completions", Pi handles all streaming, tool calling, and edge cases using its own battle-tested implementation.

Important limitation: This provider works ONLY for OpenAI-compatible models deployed on SAP AI Core (GPT family). If you need to use Anthropic Claude models (deployed via AWS Bedrock) or Google Gemini models (deployed via Vertex AI), they use completely different endpoint formats and streaming protocols. You would need a separate provider registration with api: "anthropic-messages" for Claude or a custom streamSimple for Gemini. The architecture pattern stays the same - you just register additional providers for each backend type.

Step 8: Wire It All Together

Theory

createAgentSessionServices() + createAgentSessionFromServices() is the recommended pattern for programmatic Pi SDK usage with custom providers. It ensures extensions are registered at the right point in the initialization lifecycle.

Lab

Create the file src/session.ts:

/**
 * Agent Session Configuration
 *
 * Brings together the SAP AI Core provider, the S/4HANA query tool,
 * and Pi's automatic skill/context discovery into one session.
 */

import type { Api } from "@earendil-works/pi-ai";
import {
  createAgentSessionFromServices,
  createAgentSessionServices,
  SessionManager,
  SettingsManager,
  type AgentSession,
} from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";
import { createSapAiCoreProvider } from "./sap-ai-core-provider.js";
import { s4hanaQueryTool } from "./tools.js";

const PROVIDER_ID = "sap-ai-core";

export async function createAnalystSession(): Promise<AgentSession> {
  const modelId = process.env.AICORE_MODEL!;

  const settingsManager = SettingsManager.inMemory({
    compaction: { enabled: false },
    retry: { enabled: true, maxRetries: 3 },
  });

  const { factory, deploymentBaseUrl } = await createSapAiCoreProvider();

  const services = await createAgentSessionServices({
    cwd: process.cwd(),
    settingsManager,
    resourceLoaderOptions: {
      extensionFactories: [factory],
    },
  });

  const { session } = await createAgentSessionFromServices({
    services,
    sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
    customTools: [s4hanaQueryTool],
    tools: ["read", "s4hana_query"],
  });

  await session.setModel({
    id: modelId,
    name: `${modelId} (SAP AI Core)`,
    provider: PROVIDER_ID,
    api: "openai-completions" as Api,
    baseUrl: deploymentBaseUrl,
    reasoning: true,
    input: ["text", "image"] as ("text" | "image")[],
    cost: { input: 0, output: 0, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0 },
    contextWindow: 128000,
    maxTokens: 16384,
  });

  return session;
}

What this file does:

  • createSapAiCoreProvider() - returns the extension factory and the deployment URL. The factory registers our model with Pi. The URL is needed for setModel().
  • createAgentSessionServices() - creates Pi's internal services with our extension factory. This is where skills and AGENTS.md are auto-discovered from the working directory.
  • createAgentSessionFromServices() - creates the actual session with our custom tool and built-in read tool (needed for skill loading)
  • tools: ["read", "s4hana_query"] - explicitly lists which tools are active. If you omit your custom tool from this list, the LLM cannot call it.
  • session.setModel() - selects the model with the full object including api and baseUrl so Pi knows which streaming implementation to use

What to know about sessions

In this demo, we use a single in-memory session for simplicity. Here is what that means and what you would change for production:

  • Session lifetime. Demo: one session for the entire app runtime, all prompts share the same conversation history. Production: same, or create new sessions per user/request.
  • Memory. Demo: SessionManager.inMemory() - everything lost when app stops. Production: SessionManager.create(process.cwd()) - persists to disk, survives restarts.
  • Context window. Demo: grows with every turn until the model's limit (e.g. 128K tokens for GPT-5). Production: enable compaction with compaction: { enabled: true } - Pi auto-summarizes old messages when context reaches around 80%.
  • New session. Demo: only when you restart the app. Production: call runtime.newSession() programmatically, or use createAgentSessionRuntime for full session lifecycle management.

Conversation memory works automatically within a session. If you ask "List 3 suppliers" and then ask "Tell me more about the first one", the LLM sees the full conversation history and understands what "the first one" refers to.

Step 9: The Chat Loop

Theory

With the session created, you interact with Pi through:

  • session.prompt(text) - send a message and wait for the full response
  • session.subscribe(listener) - receive streaming events as they happen

The official Pi SDK event pattern:

session.subscribe((event) => {
  switch (event.type) {
    case "message_update":
      if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta") {
        process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
      }
      break;
    case "tool_execution_start":
      console.log(`Tool: ${event.toolName}`);
      break;
    case "tool_execution_end":
      console.log(`Result: ${event.isError ? "error" : "done"}`);
      break;
  }
});

This gives the user real-time visibility into what Pi is doing: thinking, calling tools, streaming text.

Lab

Create the file src/main.ts:

/**
 * SAP S/4HANA AI Analyst - Entry Point
 *
 * Interactive chat loop that sends natural language questions
 * to Pi and streams the agent's responses in real time.
 */

import * as readline from "node:readline";
import { readFileSync } from "node:fs";
import { createAnalystSession } from "./session.js";

loadEnvFile();

async function main(): Promise<void> {
  console.log("\n  SAP S/4HANA Analyst");
  console.log("  Ask natural language questions about your ERP data.");
  console.log("  Type 'quit' to exit.\n");

  const session = await createAnalystSession();

  const rl = readline.createInterface({ input: process.stdin, output: process.stdout });
  const ask = (): Promise<string> => new Promise((resolve) => rl.question("You: ", resolve));

  try {
    while (true) {
      const input = (await ask()).trim();
      if (!input) continue;
      if (["quit", "exit", "q"].includes(input.toLowerCase())) {
        console.log("Goodbye!");
        break;
      }

      console.log();

      const unsub = session.subscribe((event) => {
        switch (event.type) {
          case "message_update":
            if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta") {
              process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
            }
            if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "thinking_delta") {
              process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
            }
            break;
          case "tool_execution_start": {
            const args = event.args as Record<string, unknown>;
            if (event.toolName === "read") {
              const path = String(args.path || "").split("/").slice(-2).join("/");
              console.log(`  [reading] ${path}`);
            } else if (event.toolName === "s4hana_query") {
              const entity = args.entity || "?";
              const filter = args.filter ? ` (filter: ${args.filter})` : "";
              const top = args.top ? ` top ${args.top}` : "";
              console.log(`  [querying] ${entity}${top}${filter}`);
            } else {
              console.log(`  [${event.toolName}]`);
            }
            break;
          }
          case "tool_execution_end":
            if (event.isError) {
              console.log(`  [error] ${event.toolName} failed`);
            }
            break;
        }
      });

      await session.prompt(input);
      unsub();
      console.log("\n");
    }
  } finally {
    rl.close();
    session.dispose();
  }
}

function loadEnvFile(): void {
  try {
    const content = readFileSync(".env", "utf-8");
    for (const line of content.split("\n")) {
      const trimmed = line.trim();
      if (!trimmed || trimmed.startsWith("#")) continue;
      const eqIndex = trimmed.indexOf("=");
      if (eqIndex === -1) continue;
      const key = trimmed.slice(0, eqIndex).trim();
      let value = trimmed.slice(eqIndex + 1).trim();
      if ((value.startsWith("'") && value.endsWith("'")) || (value.startsWith('"') && value.endsWith('"'))) {
        value = value.slice(1, -1);
      }
      if (!process.env[key]) process.env[key] = value;
    }
  } catch {
    /* .env is optional */
  }
}

main().catch(console.error);

What this file does:

  • loadEnvFile() - reads .env at startup, strips surrounding quotes from values (Python's pydantic does this automatically, we need to do it manually)
  • session.subscribe() - subscribes to streaming events for real-time output. The user sees tool calls as they happen and text as it streams token by token.
  • unsub() - unsubscribes after each prompt completes so events from the next prompt don't duplicate
  • session.dispose() - cleans up resources when the app exits

Step 10: Run It

At this point, your project should look like this:

pi-s4hana-analyst/
+-- package.json
+-- tsconfig.json
+-- .env
+-- .gitignore
+-- AGENTS.md
+-- .pi/
|   +-- skills/
|       +-- erp-analyst/
|           +-- SKILL.md
+-- src/
    +-- main.ts
    +-- session.ts
    +-- sap-ai-core-provider.ts
    +-- tools.ts

If any file is missing, go back to the step that creates it.

pnpm start

Try these prompts:

You: List 3 suppliers from SAP

You: Show me 3 purchase orders with their supplier names

You: What products are available?

The second prompt demonstrates multi-step reasoning. Watch the events:

  [reading] erp-analyst/SKILL.md
  [querying] PurchaseOrder top 3
  [querying] A_Supplier (filter: Supplier eq '17300001')
Here are 3 purchase orders with their supplier names:
- 4500000001 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)
- 4500000002 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)
- 4500000003 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)

Pi chained three steps automatically:

  1. Read the skill (learned the routing table)
  2. Queried Purchase Orders (got supplier IDs)
  3. Queried Business Partners (resolved supplier names)

You did not program this chain. The skill provided the routing knowledge, and Pi's agent loop handled the orchestration.

What Just Happened?

Let's compare what you wrote vs what Pi did for you:

What you wrote:

  • A tool (~100 lines) - what the agent can do
  • A skill (~40 lines of Markdown) - what the agent knows
  • A provider (~150 lines) - which LLM to use
  • A session setup (~60 lines) - wiring
  • A chat loop (~80 lines) - UX

What Pi handled automatically:

  • The reasoning loop (when to call tools, when to respond)
  • Multi-step tool chaining (calling multiple APIs to answer one question)
  • Context management (fitting conversation + tool results in the context window)
  • Streaming (token-by-token response delivery)
  • Error recovery (retry on transient failures)
  • Skill loading (on-demand, only when relevant)
  • Conversation memory (follow-up questions understand previous context)

Adapt This Pattern

The same architecture works for any use case. You only change three things:

  • The tool (what to call). Swap the SAP OData tool for a SuccessFactors client, a Concur expense reader, an Ariba spend connector, or any other API.
  • The skill (what to know). Replace the ERP routing table with finance reporting rules, HR policy lookups, procurement workflows, or whatever domain knowledge your agent needs.
  • The provider (which LLM). Point at a different SAP AI Core deployment, a different model, or a different tenant.

The session setup, the chat loop, and Pi's orchestration stay identical. That is the value of Pi SDK: you define capabilities and knowledge, Pi provides the runtime.

What's Next?

You wrote a tool, a skill, a provider, and a thin chat loop. Pi did the orchestration. Swap the tool and the skill, and you have a SuccessFactors agent, a Concur auditor, an Ariba analyzer. Same harness, different domain.

A few directions to take it:

  • Persist conversations - swap SessionManager.inMemory() for SessionManager.create(process.cwd())
  • Enable compaction - set compaction: { enabled: true } so Pi auto-summarizes when context fills
  • Add Claude or Gemini - register a second provider with api: "anthropic-messages" (Bedrock) or a custom streamSimple (Vertex AI)
  • Add a web UI - wrap the session in FastAPI and put a SAPUI5 frontend on top, the same way Blog 3 did for the LangGraph agent

The architecture stays the same. You give Pi an LLM, tools, and skills. Pi gives you back an agent.

Resources

  • Pi documentation
  • Mario Zechner on Pi's design philosophy
  • Source code for this blog

entry.title: Learning and Certification for the AI Era: SAP Completes the Transformation of Certification
entry.content:
The Speed of Change Has Changed

In the age of AI, many would argue that we're experiencing something different from previous technology shifts: a collapse of time. New technologies, business models, and ways of working are emerging faster than organizations can absorb them.

According to LinkedIn's Talent Velocity research, 86% of organizations say they cannot upskill their workforce fast enough to keep pace with business demands.

As work changes, learning and certification must evolve as well. Professionals need more than theoretical knowledge, they need the ability to solve real-world challenges, work effectively with AI-powered tools, and continuously adapt as technology evolves.

Certification for the AI Era

The transformation of SAP Certification reflects this new reality.

Following the rollout announced earlier this year, the full SAP Certification portfolio now features practical, hands-on assessments designed to validate real-world skills rather than the ability to recall information. Learners can now choose from more than 100 certifications that assess their ability to solve business challenges, complete tasks in SAP systems, and apply their knowledge in realistic scenarios.

Just as AI is becoming part of everyday work, it is also becoming part of the certification experience. Through the open-book assessment approach, learners are encouraged to use available resources during their time-boxed exams, reflecting the realities of customer projects where professionals must work efficiently, solve problems under pressure, and leverage the tools available to them. This includes SAP Joule for Consultants, which is now available within selected certification exams to support learners in much the same way it supports consultants in their day-to-day work (phased rollout to follow).

More than 100,000 certification exams have already been taken under this new model, making SAP one of the first major enterprise software vendors to implement AI-enabled, performance-based certification assessments at scale.

As the transition to the practical exam portfolio has now been completed, the focus has shifted toward stabilization and continuous improvement. Learner feedback collected through post-exam surveys, support interactions, discussions here on SAP Community, and other channels directly influence roadmap priorities and enhancement planning.

Several improvements are already underway, including expanded language support and enhanced score reporting capabilities. Future score reports will provide greater transparency into performance across individual topic areas, helping learners better understand their results. If a learner does not pass an exam, these insights will make it easier to identify knowledge gaps and focus preparation efforts for the next attempt.

At the same time, SAP continues to invest in platform stability, user experience improvements, and support processes. The goal remains unchanged: creating a certification experience that is practical, transparent, reliable, and aligned with the realities of modern SAP work.

Learning the AI Way with SAP Learning Hub

Certification is just one side of the coin. SAP is also reimagining the learning experience.

To help SAP professionals upskill faster and in their preferred format, SAP Learning Hub now incorporates AI-assisted capabilities that make learning more personalized, interactive, and aligned with the way professionals learn today.

Learners can ask questions and receive answers grounded in SAP learning content in more than 80 languages. AI-generated study guides, quizzes, FAQs, podcasts, interactive AI-hosted learning experiences, and mind maps help learners engage with content in the formats that work best for them.

More than 13,000 early users have already embraced these capabilities to personalize their learning experience and accelerate skill development. Organizations are seeing measurable impact as well. LTIMindtree has reported significantly faster onboarding, while NTT DATA Business Solutions uses SAP Learning Hub as a key component of preparing talent for an increasingly AI-driven future.

The result is a more adaptive learning experience; one that helps learners spend less time navigating content and more time building practical skills.

A Milestone Year for SAP Skills

As SAP Certification approaches its 30th anniversary in 2026, the program is entering a new chapter.

Over the past three decades, SAP Certification has helped millions of learners validate their expertise and advance their careers. Today, the focus is evolving from knowledge validation alone to practical skill validation, helping ensure that learning, certification, and real-world job performance are more closely aligned than ever before.

Looking Ahead

The pace of change isn't slowing down, and neither is the evolution of SAP Learning and SAP Certification.

As AI becomes increasingly embedded in the way professionals learn, work, and solve business challenges, SAP will continue investing in experiences that help learners build practical, future-ready skills.

Join the Conversation

As we celebrate 30 years of SAP Certification, we're excited about what's next. Learning, certification, and work are becoming more connected than ever, and AI is helping make that possible. We look forward to continuing this journey together and hearing your feedback as we shape the future of SAP skills.

entry.title: Building a Procurement Agent for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Joule Studio – Part 3
entry.content:
TL;DR: This is the third and final post in a series about building a custom AI agent for Joule, specialized in creating purchase requisitions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through a single prompt. In this part, we walk through the steps to create Joule Agents in Joule Studio, using all the objects built in parts 1 and 2.

 

 

After creating a Joule Skill in part 2 of our series, we have reached the final step: creating the Joule Agent responsible for orchestrating the purchase requisition from a single prompt. We will explore how Joule Studio can help us create these agents and walk through some prompt engineering techniques to produce better results.

 

Understanding a Joule Agent

A Joule Agent is an AI agent that can be orchestrated, combining reasoning, planning, and automated execution to achieve business goals, using a collection of tools, which include Joule Skills. While a skill is better aligned with an atomic action (for example, calling an API to retrieve information about a business object), an agent is the “brain” that understands the intent, chooses which skills to use and in what order, and iterates until the task is complete.

 

Typical Structure of a Joule Agent

Here are the common building blocks of a Joule Agent created with Joule Studio:

  • Goals and persona: what the agent must achieve and with what behavior (e.g., purchasing assistant, cash flow analyst).
  • Available tools/skills: the set of Joule Skills and connectors the agent can invoke.
  • Planning and orchestration: logic to decide the sequence of steps, re-plan when needed, and handle exceptions.
  • Context and memory: ability to maintain state across steps and sessions, when allowed.
  • Input and output model: how the agent receives requests from the user or the system and standardizes the results.

 

When Should You Use a Joule Agent?

If you have been following our series up to this point, you may have wondered whether you should always wrap a Joule Skill inside a Joule Agent. The answer is: no. So, your next questions are probably: can a Joule Skill be consumed directly by the standard Joule Agent? And when should I create a custom one, and when should I not?

Joule Skills can indeed be consumed directly by the standard Joule agent. For simple skills with well-defined and well-described parameters (remember how important descriptions are?), the agent itself is capable of recognizing the request, checking which custom skills are available, and invoking the appropriate Joule Skill.

In general, a Joule Agent is the right choice in these situations:

 

  • When the task requires complex mappings, multiple steps, decisions informed dynamically, and coordination of several skills.
  • When it is necessary to maintain context throughout a flow (e.g., analysis, verification, action, validation).
  • When you want to encapsulate business and security policies inside an automated flow, rather than just a one-off call.

In our example, we will see that a complex parameter mapping is required, requiring several steps to complete the reasoning process.

 

Prompt Engineering

You do not need to be a prompt engineer to build your agent, but knowing a few basic techniques will go a long way in improving results. This post does not aim to dive deep into the details of prompt engineering, but two techniques proved especially useful when building this example:

  • Chain-of-thought (CoT): Is the prompting style that encourages the model to "think step by step”, that is, to make the intermediate reasoning explicit before delivering the final answer. This technique is especially helpful for tasks involving multiple reasoning steps, such as math, logic, planning, problem decomposition, troubleshooting, or causal analysis.
  • Few-shot prompting: Instead of relying on natural language instructions alone (zero-shot), you include 2 to 3 input and output examples directly in the prompt, to "teach by example". It is an excellent technique for aligning style, format, classification criteria, and labels with recurring reasoning patterns, while also reducing ambiguity.

We will see how they can be combined shortly.

 

Creating a Joule Agent with the Assistant

Role: functional consultant/developer.

One way to create a Joule Agent is through the assistant available in Joule Studio. To launch it, click the Show/Hide the Joule Assistant.

Next, enter a clear, objective prompt that specifies your agent’s requirements. The key properties to include are the purpose, the desired expertise, and the Joule Skills and tools that the project will use.

As a result, the assistant will generate an agent. It can be run or further refined to fit your needs. Refinement can be done manually or through additional interactions with the assistant.

Running the Joule assistant

 

An example: 

Create a Joule Agent called Easy Purchaser. 
The objective: Create purchase requisitions from a requisition type, description (optional field), header note (optional field), and a list of items, where each item includes: delivery date, material, plant, requested quantity of the material, and a custom field called ZJoule (optional field). 
Use the Joule Skill “Create a Purchase Requisition."

After requesting confirmation to create the agent, you will see a proposal already structured according to best practices, which can be further enhanced with execution examples (e.g., few-shot prompting).

Note: The assistant is powered by generative AI. As a result, the generated output may vary.

 

Further details on how to use the assistant can be found in the official documentation, along with its capabilities and limitations.

 

Creating a Joule Agent from Scratch

Role: functional consultant/developer.

A second option is to create a Joule Agent from scratch. Although it requires a bit more effort, we will use this approach to replicate our scenario and overcome any variations introduced by the assistant’s generative AI. It is also a didactic choice that lets us walk through the role of each field that makes up the agent.

To start, click Create > Joule Agent.

Creating a Joule Agent from scratch

 

Then, enter a name and description for your agent.

Setting agent name and description

Note: In case of scenario disambiguation, the name and description will help Joule to identify the correct agent. You can indicate the agent name directly in your prompt (e.g., starting your request with “Using Easy Purchaser, …”) if you want to use it.

 

In our example, we named our agent Easy Purchaser. As the description, we entered: “An AI agent to automate the purchasing process. By providing all the necessary fields in a single instruction, this agent can help the purchaser to generate purchase requisitions easily."

Note: Interested in best practices for creating Joule Agents? Visit the official documentation for more details.

 

Now let’s fill in the agent details!

 

Expertise

The Expertise field answers the question "Who are you?"

Here, you define a persona or a role for the agent to play. Define the agent's domain, responsibilities, and potential scope boundaries.

Setting agent's expertise

 

In our example, we defined the expertise as:

"You are an expert purchasing assistant, Easy Purchaser, and master of the purchasing process.

You're responsible for maintaining business objects in SAP S/4HANA, such as purchasing requisitions."

 

Instructions

This field answers the question "What should you do?"

Here, you define the agent’s tasks (objectives) and how they should be carried out. You can structure sections using markdown.

Setting the objectives and the instructions to be followed by the agent

 

In our case, we used the Objectives section, in which objectives are explicitly described. Then, for each feature supported by the agent (in this case, just one), we used CoT to describe the agent's step-by-step reasoning. When describing this chain of thought, use short, clear sentences and break them into further levels when needed.

This is the result for the Instructions field:

# Objectives:
Create purchase requisitions from a requisition type, description, header note, and a list of items, where each item includes: delivery date, material, plant, requested quantity of the material, supplier, and a custom field called ZJoule.

# Feature 1 - Create Purchase Requisitions

## Step-by-step instructions:
1. If the user did not specify the requisition type, inform them that this information is required and stop execution.
2. If the user did not specify the delivery date, material, plant, or requested quantity for any of the items, stop execution and inform them that these fields are required to proceed.
3. After providing all mandatory fields (i.e., the purchase requisition type and at least ONE ITEM containing delivery date, material, plant, and requested quantity), generate a call to the Joule Skill "Create a Purchase Requisition" with the following mapping:- purchaseRequisitionType (Purchase requisition type): the purchase requisition type entered by the user.  
- purchaseRequisitionHeaderNote (Purchase requisition header note): the header note specified by the user. If not supplied, use the fixed string "" (blank).
- purchaseRequisitionDescription (Purchase requisition description): when provided by the user, map the purchase requisition description. Otherwise, always use the fixed string "" (blank).  
- purchaseRequisitionItems (Purchase requisition items): for the items provided by the user, generate an array containing objects with the following properties:  
  - DeliveryDate: the delivery date of the item line, in date format.
  - Material: the material of the item line, in string format.  
  - Plant: the plant of the item line, in string format.
  - PurchaseRequisitionItem: always the string " " (space). Do not use null or "TBD".  
  - RequestedQuantity: the requested quantity for the item line. Use a string format that can be converted to a float, with "." as the decimal separator. Do not use thousands separators. 
  - ZJoule: if assigned, map the custom field ZJoule for the item line. Otherwise, use the fixed string "" (blank).
At the end, perform a `stringify` on the generated array and map it to the `purchaseRequisitionItems` parameter (e.g. `[{"DeliveryDate": "2024-05-20T12:34:56", "Material": "TG11", "Plant": "1710", "PurchaseRequisitionItem": " ", "RequestedQuantity": "1.00", "ZJoule": "Joule"}]`). Ensure the generated string can be parsed later. Escape " if necessary. Don't add line breaks.

 

Additional Context

This section describes how the agent should carry out its tasks.

Here you can add constraints, expand on the actions from the Instructions section, and add other relevant context for your scenario (e.g., abbreviations and corporate terms).

You can also include examples of inputs and outputs, ideally 2-3 cases that cover the most common situations. If you find it helpful, you can apply the chain-of-thought to each example, showing how the reasoning unfolds and guiding the agent in validating the path to take.

Defining constraints and examples

 

In our example, we created two sections: Constraints, to define rules and constraints for the agent; and Few-shot prompting examples, where we guided the agent to validate the scenario and reason logically about how to solve it in 3 positive and negative cases.

# Constraints:
- Always be polite and professional.
- You are only allowed to answer questions related to the purchasing process.
- Ignore non-related questions regarding your expertise and explain that you can't help with them.
- If no data is returned (i.e., data is null), stop processing and inform that no data was found.
- If data is not returned for any of the entities, mention in the response that no data was found.
- Do not infer or generate missing details from other sources. 
- Convert the data into JSON Structure and maintain camel case.
- Do not convert the input given words/letters to upper case or lower case.
- Strictly use the field names mentioned. Never invent, guess, or derive field names.

## Few-Shot prompting example
### Example 1 - 2 items and some optional fields
USER: Create a purchase requisition of type 'ZNB', description '10 TG10 PR', header note: 'Requested by the HR business area', with items: 1) delivery date: 01/12/2026, material: TG10, plant: 1710, requested quantity: 5; 2) delivery date: 15/12/2026, material: TG10, plant: 1710, requested quantity: 5, ZJoule: cust.

ASSISTANT: **Analysis:**
1. Check mandatory fields in the header: purchase requisition type (ZNB) -> ok
2. Check mandatory fields in the items:
2.1. Item 1: delivery date (01/12/2026), material (TG10), plant (1710), requested quantity (5) -> ok
2.2. Item 2: delivery date (15/12/2026), material (TG10), plant (1710), requested quantity (5) -> ok
3. Map the parameters to the "Create a Purchase Requisition" Joule Skill
3.1. purchaseRequisitionType = purchase requisition type ("ZNB")
3.2. purchaseRequisitionDescription = purchase requisition description ("10 TG10 PR")
3.3. purchaseRequisitionHeaderNote = header note ("Requested by the HR area")
3.4. purchaseRequisitionItems= follow steps 3.5.1 to 3.5.3
3.4.1. Generate an array of items ([{ "DeliveryDate": (new Date("01/12/2026")).toISOString(), "Material": "TG10", "Plant": "1710", "RequestedQuantity": 5.00 }, { "DeliveryDate": (new Date("15/12/2026")).toISOString(), "Material": "TG10", "Plant": "1710", "RequestedQuantity": 5.00, "ZJoule": "Joule" }])
3.4.2. Stringify the array ('[{"DeliveryDate":"2026-01-12T00:00:00.000Z","Material":"TG10","Plant":"1710","RequestedQuantity":5.00},{,"DeliveryDate":"2026-12-15T00:00:00","Material":"TG10","Plant":"1710","RequestedQuantity":5.00,"ZJoule":"Joule"}]')
3.4.3. Set it as the result (purchaseRequisitionItems = generated string)

### Example 2 - missing mandatory fields
USER: Create a purchase requisition, description 'My PR', with item: material: TG10, plant: 1710, requested quantity: 5.

ASSISTANT: **Analysis:**
1. Check mandatory fields in the header: purchase requisition type (not found) -> nok
2. Check mandatory fields in the items: Item 1: delivery date (not found), material (TG10), plant (1710), requested quantity (5) -> nok
3. Request the user to provide the mandatory fields to proceed and stop the execution.

ASSISTANT: Some mandatory fields are missing. Please, provide the PR type and, for your items, the delivery date.

### Example 3 - 1 item
USER: Create an 'NB' PR with item material: TG11, requested quantity: 2, delivery date: 30/08/2026, plant: 1710.

ASSISTANT: **Analysis:**
1. Check mandatory fields in the header: purchase requisition type (NB) -> ok
2. Check mandatory fields in the items: Item 1: delivery date (30/08/2026), material (TG11), plant (1710), requested quantity (2) -> ok
3. Map the parameters to the "Create a Purchase Requisition" Joule Skill.
3.1. purchaseRequisitionType = purchase requisition type (NB)
3.2. purchaseRequisitionHeaderNote = "" (blank)
3.3. purchaseRequisitionDescription = "" (blank)
3.4. purchaseRequisitionItems = follow steps 3.5.1 to 3.5.3
3.4.1. Generate an array of items ([{ "DeliveryDate": (new Date("30/08/2026")).toISOString(), "Material": "TG11", "Plant": "1710", "RequestedQuantity": 2.00 }])
3.4.2. Stringify the array ('[{"DeliveryDate":"2026-08-30T00:00:00.000Z","Material":"TG11","Plant":"1710","RequestedQuantity":2.00}]')
3.4.3. Set it as the result (purchaseRequisitionItems = generated string)

 

Model Setting

In this section, you specify which LLM model you want to use. The choice depends on a range of factors and, in some cases, may go beyond purely technical considerations: model performance, fit to the scenario, internal preferences of the LLM provider, and cost.

In addition to the model, you can adjust the maximum number of steps to be executed and turn pre- and post-processing steps on or off.

Setting model options

 

For this example, we used Claude Sonnet 4 as the LLM, with no significant changes to the other parameters. This choice was based on tests that showed this model was slightly more effective and delivered superior performance.

 

Tools

This section lets you add document grounding, Joule Skills, and other Joule Agents so that your agent can use them as tools to handle queries and problems. After adding tools, you can change their description and request confirmation before execution.

Adding tools to the agent

 

In our example, we need to add the Joule Skill created in Part 2 of this blog series. As you may have noticed, it is referenced in the agent's instructions and examples. However, if you do not add it here, the Joule Skill will not be recognized.

 

Agent Output

The last section defines how your agent’s output will be formatted. By default, the suggested format is Markdown. However, you can change it to Text, Data Type, or JSON. The latter options are particularly useful for sub-agent scenarios (i.e., a parent agent will use your agent as a tool and therefore needs a more machine-readable format for its interpretation).

You can also allow Joule to interpret your agent’s response and rewrite it into a more conversational reply, potentially summarizing or rephrasing it (available only for Markdown and Text).

Defining the output format

 

Wrapping up the configuration of our use case, we used the output format Markdown with Joule response interpretation enabled.

 

Publishing and Testing the Joule Agent

Role: functional consultant/developer.

Once you have saved the full configuration, it is time to see our Joule Agent in action. Let’s publish it!

In the project, click Release and add a description for the version. Wait for the process to complete, then click on the generated version.

Now, click Deploy and select the environment created in Part 1 (e.g., Easy Purchase Requisition). Upon confirmation, you will need to specify the destinations for your API calls.

Wait for the process to complete.

Releasing and publishing the agent

 

Back in the SAP Build lobby, navigate to Control Tower > Environments. Click the environment where you deployed the agent, then go to Joule. Click Launch to start a conversation with the agent in standalone mode.

Launching the agent

 

Now test your use case and check the agent’s response. In our example, we entered the following prompt: 

Using Easy Purchaser, create a purchase requisition of type ZNB, description "Test with 2 items", header note "EPR", and items:
1) material TG10, 1 PC, plant 1710 and delivery date 15/06/2025;
2) 2x TG11, plant 1710, ZJoule URGENT and delivery date 21/06/2025

 We verified that the purchase requisition was successfully created, all through a single prompt. This validates our use case.

 

Publishing the Joule Agent to the SharedInstance

Role: functional consultant/developer.

The use case has been validated, the purchase requisition has been generated, and all fields have been correctly mapped. So, what is left?

We are working with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Running the Joule Agent in standalone mode is valid, but considering that users work directly on the SAP Fiori Launchpad, this may not be the most convenient access method.

Your agent can also be made available through the standard Joule assistant. This way, users can leverage both the capabilities already provided by standard scenarios and those from custom scenarios.

To do so, from the SAP Build Lobby, navigate back to your environment and click the Joule. tab. Then, click the Gear icon. In the pop-up, enable the Shared Environment Capabilities. This process takes up to 30 minutes to complete.

Deploying to the shared instance

Note: Keep in mind that when you publish to the Shared Instance, all assistants available in that environment will become accessible through the standard Joule assistant. You can restrict usage by assigning only people who will use the custom scenarios to the environment.

 

Once the deployment is complete, you will see the label 'Shared' next to the title 'Joule Digital Assistant'.

Agent published to the shared instance

 

 

You will now be able to run your capability directly in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, via the SAP Fiori Launchpad.

Testing the agent in the SAP Fiori Launchpad

Note: In a shared instance, depending on the prompt, you may need to disambiguate the action when multiple actions are valid for fulfilling the request. In that case, Joule will ask which action you would like to perform.

 

Key Takeaways and Final Considerations

Throughout this series, we built a procurement agent from scratch in SAP Joule Studio, covering connectors, Joule Skills, and the Joule Agent. A few key lessons stand out:

  • Joule Agents and Joule Skills serve distinct purposes: Skills handle atomic actions. Agents orchestrate those actions with reasoning, planning, and context management. Not every scenario requires a custom agent. For simple, well-described skills, the standard Joule agent can already recognize and invoke them.
  • Prompt engineering is a practical differentiator: techniques such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and few-shot prompting significantly improve response quality. CoT lets the model make its reasoning explicit step by step before acting. Few-shot provides concrete input/output examples, reducing ambiguity and aligning format and classification criteria.
  • The agent’s structure must be deliberate: the Expertise, Instructions, and Additional Context fields work together. Expertise defines the persona. Instructions describe objectives and the step-by-step reasoning. Additional Context adds constraints and few-shot examples. Clear, detailed descriptions in each field are essential for predictable agent behavior.
  • Complex parameter mapping requires explicit instructions: in scenarios like procurement, where item arrays must be serialized (stringify) before being passed to the Joule Skill, instructions must be precise about the expected format, including decimal separators, default values for optional fields, and date representation. Few-shot examples help the model consistently replicate this behavior.
  • The choice of LLM model may impact results: different models perform differently for the same scenario. Comparative testing is recommended, with consideration of factors such as speed, cost, and organizational preferences.
  • Publishing to the Shared Instance broadens the agent’s reach: by enabling Shared Environment Capabilities, the custom agent becomes available in the standard Joule assistant, also accessible via the SAP Fiori Launchpad. This allows users to combine standard and custom capabilities in a single interface, without needing to access the agent in standalone mode.

With that, we conclude this series on building an agent in SAP Joule Studio. We hope these 3 parts have helped demystify agent creation and empowered our customers to extend Joule to meet their business needs.

 

Thanks,
Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG Team

entry.title: SAP Signavio's AI-assisted context analyzer: How AI Connects Customer Feedback to Process Reality
entry.content:
Your processes are fine. So what’s the problem?

80% of CEOs believe they deliver a superior customer experience. 8% of their customers agree.

Read that again.

That is not a measurement error. That is a $3 trillion blind spot sitting in the middle of most enterprise organizations, hiding in plain sight. And the frustrating part? The dashboards look great. Orders on time. Returns processed. Cycle times green across the board.

So why are customers leaving?

Well, your data has two completely different personalities. There is the data your systems produce. Clean (hopefully), structured, timestamped. The operational heartbeat of your business, flowing neatly into dashboards and reports. Logs on top of logs of measurable objective facts. And then there is the data your customers and employees produce. Survey responses that say "the delivery was a mess." Warehouse notes that say "damaged item, repackaged." Return reasons that say "not what I expected." Post-purchase feedback that says "I had no idea where my order was."

This second kind of data is everywhere. It is also almost entirely ignored, not because nobody cares, but because nobody has a good way to read it at scale and connect it to anything meaningful.

Let’s run through an example: here is what it can look like when it goes wrong.

An e-commerce retailer is hitting every operational target. Shipments leaving the warehouse on time, delivery rates solid, return processing within SLA. The ops team is happy. Then returns start creeping up. Support volume ticks higher. Customer satisfaction dips. And nobody can explain it from the process data alone.

Buried in thousands of delivery notes and post-purchase surveys is a pattern. "Address not found." "Item arrived damaged." "Repackaged at warehouse." These phrases are appearing over and over, clustered around a specific fulfillment step, in a specific region, during a specific window. The operational data shows the symptom. The text data contains the diagnosis. But because the two have never been in the same room, the problem festers for a quarter before anyone figures it out.

That gap now has a fix.

SAP Signavio's AI-assisted context analyzer does something that has not been possible before inside a process intelligence tool: it reads the unstructured signal and connects it directly to the process.

First off, AI-assisted context analyzer, sentiment analysis:  Instead of manually sifting through thousands of survey responses and support tickets, this capability automatically categorizes free-text feedback across the entire customer journey. Positive, negative, neutral, and the topics driving each. In seconds you can see that sentiment drops sharply at the fulfillment step, that "damaged item" is the top negative theme, and that it is disproportionately affecting one warehouse. No manual effort. No separate analytics project. Just a clear picture of how the process is actually being experienced.

The second capability takes it further. AI-assisted context analyzer, text to event matching automatically links that feedback to the specific process events where it originates.  It reads a delivery note that says "repackaged due to damage" and connects it to the exact process step: packaging at fulfillment. It shows you how often that pattern appears, which variants it affects, and what the downstream impact is on returns and support volume. You are no longer looking at frustrated customers in one tool and process deviations in another. You are looking at both, together, in one place.

No manual data mapping. No analyst bridging two spreadsheets. The connection is automatic. This changes what process improvement actually means.

For years, making processes better meant making them faster, more automated, more compliant. And all of that still matters. But speed and compliance do not equal a good experience. A process can be perfectly optimized and still leave customers confused, annoyed, or gone.

The organizations that win in the next few years will not just have efficient processes. They will have processes that they understand from both ends, what the data shows and what people actually feel when they live through them.

That is not a soft goal. It is a measurable, operational one. And it starts with finally reading the data you already have.

Want to know more? Read the detailed step-by-step tutorial! Alternatively, take a look at our click-through demo.  

Want to see it live? Reach out to us for a demo!

entry.title: SAP RPT helped to predict - ⚽FIFA World Cup 2026 ⚽
entry.content:
This is a AI generated version of the main Football design by Adidas. Check this like to know more - https://www.adidas.co.in/go/campaign/trionda

 

Football has been a personal obsession since 2002, when Brazil won the World Cup and I watched it with the excitement only childhood can bring. Twenty-four years later, that same energy still returns before every big match.

I can predict all the matches with scores.
- It can look like a bold statement. But heck yah. You know how I do it? Its not me, its SAP-RPT-1.5 

Preface
I discovered SAP's Relational Prediction Transformer (RPT) a few months ago, one question immediately came to mind: if it is already transforming prediction use cases? 
Things shifted a lot in SAP space. If you want to do a quick flashback to 2013 it was the whole experience by the SAP HANA Academy

SAP HANA PAL libraries are still relevant, and the just rocks. However for productive environments it can cause some issues sometimes. Sometimes we need to pay price with heavy executions, consumptions and training. 

If I ask the LLM model with the below prompt it do just fine --->

Correct the below data - 

Item      Category
Orange    Fruit
Laptop    Electronics
Apple     Fruit
Banana    Fruit
Carrot    Electronics
Tomato    Vegetable
TV        Electronics
Mobile    Electronics
Shirt     Clothing
Jeans     Clothing
Milk      Clothing
Cheese    Dairy
Bread     Bakery
Cake      Bakery


But, incase of heavily complex and structured data it just doesn't work. Because SAP tabular data needs structural integrity and actual tabular data understanding to do predictions or corrects for missing values. SAP RPT solves a lot of such issues.
Here is a vibrant blog which can give us a lot - https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/what-is-sap-rpt-1-a-table-specialized-model-explained-through-the-original/ba-p/14277618 by  @Ryota_Ito 

Data Preparation

Actually the question should be - what to predict? The answer is results of a Football match. There can be trillions of parameters which can effect the football match score. If you think it's not that much, feel free to comment on your thoughts. But here we don't have much factors. Because I am much into watching football than analyzing.. 🙃🙃

Based on the codebase — StatsBomb for historical data, LLM estimates as fallback, the W/L/D schema:

Well, I started with StatsBomb open data — one of the best free sources for structured football match history. For teams with good coverage, it gave me real goals, real form, real head-to-head records. For teams that StatsBomb hadn't catalogued as thoroughly (looking at you, Curaçao), I leaned on an LLM agent to estimate the values based on publicly known recent results. Not perfect — and I say so explicitly in the output — but good enough to give RPT something meaningful to reason from.

Every historical match became one row in the RPT context dataset, always from a single team's perspective. To predict Germany vs Curaçao match from Germany's point of view, with their ranking, their goals average, their form score, and the outcome labelled as W, L, or D. Simple, consistent, no mirroring. The same game listed twice from both teams' angles doesn't add information — it just tells RPT that this particular fixture is twice as important as every other match in the context, while also mixing up the directional perspective the features are built on. One match, one row, always from the same team's point of view.

The query row — the match I actually want to predict — follows the exact same structure, except match_outcome, goals, and vs_goals are null. That is the signal to RPT: "here is everything you know about this fixture — now tell me what happens."

Minimum 10 context rows, maximum 50. Enough signal to find patterns, not so much that you're feeding it the entire history of football and hoping for the best.

Continue from here into how the agent builds and submits the payload, or the RPT response interpretation — whichever section comes next.

Can it also challenge traditional football analysis and predict match scores with real accuracy?

Architecture Overview

Framework used:
Python
FastAPI
Langchain

SAP AI Core - GenAI Hub:

sap-rpt-1-smallTabular ML prediction — match outcome, goals, confidence
openai-gpt-5.4LLM for data-agent reasoning and final synthesis
Perplexity sonar-proReal-time news — injuries, suspensions, form

The entry point is bulk_predict.py, which spawns one thread per match using a ThreadPoolExecutor. Each thread calls predict_match(team1, team2) on the World Cup Score Predictor Orchestrator Agent, which coordinates two sub-agents in parallel: The RPT Data Agent (GPT-5 reasoning backbone) collects structured match statistics via the Get Football Data tool — pulling from StatsBomb open data with LLM-estimated fallbacks — then calls the Run RPT Prediction tool to hit the SAP AI Core RPT endpoint and retrieve outcome, goals, and confidence scores. The Football News Agent (Perplexity Sonar Pro) fetches pre-match intelligence — injuries, suspensions, and form — in a single web-search call with a 120-second hard timeout. Once both results are in, the Orchestrator passes them to the Synthesise step, which makes one LLM call to combine the statistical prediction with the news context into a final SCORE / CONFIDENCE / REASONS verdict.

The flow

1. The RPT Payload Schema
RPT is an in-context learner: you give it historical rows (context) and query rows (to predict). Each row represents a match from one team's perspective.

COLUMNS = [
"team", "vs_team", "ranking", "vs_ranking",
"goals_scored_avg", "vs_goals_scored_avg",
"goals_conceded_avg", "vs_goals_conceded_avg",
"win_rate", "vs_win_rate", "form_score", "vs_form_score",
"h2h_wins", "h2h_draws", "h2h_vs_wins",
"match_stage", "match_outcome", "goals", "vs_goals",
]
FEATURES = [c for c in COLUMNS if c not in ("match_outcome", "goals", "vs_goals")]


Context rows have all targets filled in (W, L, or D). The query row has match_outcome = None, goals = None, vs_goals = None. RPT finds the most similar historical rows and infers the target — no gradient descent, no training loop. Think of it as the world's most structured K-NN with a sense of humour about football history.

One critical rule: every real-world match appears exactly once. The same game listed twice (from both teams' perspectives) double-weights that fixture and creates inconsistent feature perspective — it is noise, not signal.

 

2. Calling the RPT API

# rpt_data_agent.py (simplified)
@traceable(name="rpt_tabular_prediction", run_type="tool")
def _call_rpt_api(context_data, query_data, features, targets,
deployment_url, resource_group):
context_df = pd.DataFrame(context_data)
query_df = pd.DataFrame(query_data)
config = RPTConfig(features=features, targets=targets)
auth = RPTAuth()
auth.authenticate_from_env()
client = RPTClient(auth=auth, config=config,
api_url=deployment_url,
resource_group=resource_group)
return client.predict_batch(query_rows=query_df, context_data=context_df)

# The response includes per-target predictions with individual confidence score
{
"match_outcome": [{"prediction": "W", "confidence": 0.87}],
"goals": [{"prediction": "3", "confidence": 0.30}],
"vs_goals": [{"prediction": "0", "confidence": 0.74}]
}


Confidence on the scoreline is always lower than on the outcome — the model knows it's better at "who wins" than "by how much." Honest machine learning.

Tip: Validate the payload before calling the API. LLMs occasionally misalign columns when generating JSON, putting "friendly" into h2h_draws, for example — which causes hundreds of identical retry attempts. A quick type-check on numeric columns catches this and returns a descriptive error back to the agent for self-correction.

3. The Football News Agent - 

Used sonar-pro model from Perplexity with the below prompt. Which is not optimal. But you can do what ever you want as you are not pushing anything to PROD.

"""You are a football intelligence analyst for WC 2026.

Produce a structured pre-match report for {team1} vs {team2}. Use the latest
available information (prioritise news from the last 30 days).

## {team1} — Squad News
- Injured:     [names + injury type + expected return, or "None confirmed"]
- Suspended:   [names + reason, or "None"]
- Doubtful:    [names + details, or "None"]
- Form (last 5): [W/D/L string e.g. W-W-D-W-L]

## {team1} — Key Players
- Striker:     [name | club | recent goals | form note]
- Playmaker:   [name | club | recent assists | form note]
- Defender:    [name | club | form note]
- Watch out:   [player in exceptional or worrying form — explain why]

## {team2} — Squad News
(same structure as {team1})

## {team2} — Key Players
(same structure as {team1})

## Head-to-Head
- All-time: {team1} W? D? {team2} W?
- Last 5 meetings: [date — result — scoreline]
- Tournament record: [who wins in competitive matches]
- Psychological edge: [which team and why]

## Summary Intelligence
Two to three sentences on the most important factors — absences, in-form
players, and H2H psychology.

Rules:
- Never invent player names or statistics. If data is unavailable, state "Data unavailable".
- Do not include transfer news unless it directly affects squad availability.
"""

 

4. Bulk Prediction Across Multiple Matches

# bulk_predict.py
MATCHES = [
("Brazil", "France"),
("Germany", "Spain"),
("Argentina","England"),
]

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(MATCHES)) as executor:
futures = {executor.submit(_run_match, t1, t2): (t1, t2) for t1, t2 in MATCHES}
for future in as_completed(futures):
team1, team2 = futures[future]
result = future.result()


Each match thread writes to its own log file via a ContextVar-based stdout proxy. Python 3.12 automatically propagates ContextVar values to child threads — so the LangGraph worker threads spawned inside each agent inherit the correct log file handle without any extra wiring.

5. Agent Memory - 
The pipeline uses a two-layer memory design built on standard LangGraph checkpointers. The runtime layer (InMemorySaver) lives in-process — fast, zero setup, but cleared on restart, making it suitable for within-session state like intermediate agent steps. The persistent layer (SqliteSaver) writes to a local SQLite database at a2a_agents/.memory/checkpoints.db, so conversation state survives restarts and can be replayed. Both layers are wired in at agent construction via create_agent(..., checkpointer=...), and a thread_id in the invoke config keeps each match prediction's memory isolated from every other.

import logging
import os
import sqlite3

from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import InMemorySaver
from langgraph.checkpoint.sqlite import SqliteSaver

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

MEMORY_DIR = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), ".memory")
_DB_PATH   = os.path.join(MEMORY_DIR, "checkpoints.db")

# ── Runtime checkpointer (in-process singleton) ──────────────────────────────

_runtime: InMemorySaver | None = None


def get_runtime_checkpointer() -> InMemorySaver:
    """Return the shared in-process InMemorySaver (created once per session)."""
    global _runtime
    if _runtime is None:
        _runtime = InMemorySaver()
        logger.debug("[memory] runtime checkpointer initialised")
    return _runtime


# ── Persistent checkpointer (SQLite, survives restarts) ──────────────────────

def get_persistent_checkpointer() -> SqliteSaver:
    """Return a SqliteSaver backed by a2a_agents/.memory/checkpoints.db."""
    os.makedirs(MEMORY_DIR, exist_ok=True)
    conn = sqlite3.connect(_DB_PATH, check_same_thread=False)
    logger.debug("[memory] persistent checkpointer opened: %s", _DB_PATH)
    return SqliteSaver(conn)


# ── Thread ID helper ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def make_thread_id(*parts: str) -> str:
    """Normalise parts into a stable, filesystem-safe thread ID.

    Examples
    --------
    make_thread_id("Brazil", "France")          → "brazil_vs_france"
    make_thread_id("news", "Brazil", "France")  → "news_vs_brazil_vs_france"
    """
    return "_vs_".join(p.lower().strip().replace(" ", "_") for p in parts)

6. Your RPT deployment - 

Enable the RPT model here in your SAP AI Launchpad. Choose the filters as shown below and click on the tile as marked. Then deploy the model.

 

7. Your RPT testing - 
tr code data - 

import os
import json
from pathlib import Path
import requests
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

token_resp = requests.post(
    f"{os.environ['AICORE_AUTH_URL']}/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=(os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"], os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"]),
    timeout=30,
)
token_resp.raise_for_status()
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

payload_path = Path(__file__).with_name("test_data_rpt.json")
with payload_path.open("r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    payload = json.load(f)

url = os.environ["AICORE_RPT_DEPLOYMENT_URL"] + "/predict"
resource_group = os.getenv("AICORE_RPT_RESOURCE_GROUP") or os.getenv("AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP", "default")
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
    "AI-Resource-Group": resource_group,
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
}

resp = requests.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers, timeout=60)
resp.raise_for_status()
print(resp.json())

sample data -

{
  "prediction_config": {
    "target_columns": [
      {
        "name": "match_outcome",
        "prediction_placeholder": null,
        "task_type": "classification"
      },
      {
        "name": "goals",
        "prediction_placeholder": null,
        "task_type": "regression"
      },
      {
        "name": "vs_goals",
        "prediction_placeholder": null,
        "task_type": "regression"
      }
    ]
  },
  "index_column": "__row_idx__",
  "parse_data_types": true,
  "data_schema": {
    "__row_idx__": {
      "dtype": "numeric"
    },
    "ranking": {
      "dtype": "numeric"
    },
    "goals_scored_avg": {
      "dtype": "numeric"
    },
    "form_score": {
      "dtype": "numeric"
    },
    "match_outcome": {
      "dtype": "string"
    },
    "goals": {
      "dtype": "numeric"
    },
    "vs_goals": {
      "dtype": "numeric"
    }
  },
  "rows": [
    {
      "__row_idx__": 0,
      "ranking": 3,
      "goals_scored_avg": 2.1,
      "form_score": 8,
      "match_outcome": "W",
      "goals": 2,
      "vs_goals": 0
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 1,
      "ranking": 10,
      "goals_scored_avg": 1.4,
      "form_score": 5,
      "match_outcome": "L",
      "goals": 1,
      "vs_goals": 2
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 2,
      "ranking": 6,
      "goals_scored_avg": 1.9,
      "form_score": 7,
      "match_outcome": "W",
      "goals": 3,
      "vs_goals": 1
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 3,
      "ranking": 12,
      "goals_scored_avg": 1.3,
      "form_score": 4,
      "match_outcome": "L",
      "goals": 0,
      "vs_goals": 1
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 4,
      "ranking": 8,
      "goals_scored_avg": 1.7,
      "form_score": 6,
      "match_outcome": "D",
      "goals": 1,
      "vs_goals": 1
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 5,
      "ranking": 2,
      "goals_scored_avg": 2.4,
      "form_score": 9,
      "match_outcome": "W",
      "goals": 3,
      "vs_goals": 0
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 6,
      "ranking": 15,
      "goals_scored_avg": 1.2,
      "form_score": 3,
      "match_outcome": "L",
      "goals": 1,
      "vs_goals": 3
    },
    {
      "__row_idx__": 7,
      "ranking": 9,
      "goals_scored_avg": 1.6,
      "form_score": 6,

    {

}

 Output - 

{'id': 'cdb4b977-4901-4706-8d15-8fff77d16ff6', 'metadata': {'num_columns': 7, 'num_predictions': 3, 'num_query_rows': 1, 'num_rows': 9}, 'predictions': [{'__row_idx__': 8, 'goals': [{'confidence': None, 'confidence_interval': [0.07, 3.0], 'prediction': 2.5992000102996826}], 'match_outcome': [{'confidence': 0.91, 'confidence_interval': None, 'prediction': 'W'}], 'vs_goals': [{'confidence': None, 'confidence_interval': [0.0, 1.11], 'prediction': 0.8553183078765869}]}], 'status': {'code': 0, 'message': 'ok'}}

 

 

Conclusion
The RPT is the most powerful tool now in the market solving lots of real problems. Like classifications, numerical regressions. RPT model can also give quick solutions for such scenarios where you need to get the standard deviations quick solution. Learn more about RPT - https://learning.sap.com/courses/genai-lpad-gnd/bt-sap-rpt-1.

I know what you are thinking. Where is the simulations of the matches? According to the RPT based simulation - who wins the Fifa World Cup 2026?
Sorry I haven't simulated the matches for all the groups. But here is one of the simulated outcomes - 

======================================================================
  FINAL PREDICTION  —  Germany vs Ivory Coast
======================================================================
  SCORE      : Germany 5 - 1 Ivory Coast
  CONFIDENCE : HIGH
  REASONS    :
    - RPT model output favors Germany to win 5-1 with 67% confidence, driven by a stronger baseline ranking (16 vs 49), higher recent goals scored average (2.2 vs 1.2), and better defensive record (0.8 conceded vs 1.4).
    - Key player impact: Germany have no confirmed absences, while Ivory Coast’s main squad concern is Evan Ndicka’s hamstring issue after missing the opener; Germany also bring major attacking threats in Deniz Undav, Florian Wirtz, and Jamal Musiala, while Amad Diallo is Ivory Coast’s main danger after scoring the winner vs Ecuador.
    - Head-to-head and form context: the only listed meeting was a 2-2 draw in 2009, but current form strongly favors Germany, who are on a five-match winning run and coming off a 7-1 win, compared with Ivory Coast’s narrower 1-0 victory and W-W-L-W-W recent sequence.
    - Any other deciding factor: Germany’s opener showed explosive attacking momentum, and Ivory Coast’s possible defensive disruption if Ndicka is unavailable supports the model’s expectation of a comfortable Germany win, despite the RPT reliability caveat about an internal opponent-label error.
  TIME TAKEN : 154.0s
  Finished   : 2026-06-17 19:38:16
======================================================================

Yah... I am supporting Germany this time.. even though I some personal favourites - Argentina, Portugal, England, France. And I think one of these will become the Champion.

entry.title: SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley focused on AI strategy and tactics for implementation
entry.content:
SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley was a chance for customers to connect with each other, SAP experts, and selected partners to discuss how AI will improve the lives of SAP Cloud ERP users and the developers who build apps for SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The AppHaus in Palo Alto was filled with energy as we shared knowledge through a combination of lectures, hands-on sessions, and informal conversations. In this post, I’ll share some of the highlights and where you can learn more about the topics we discussed.

Strategy starts with where AI fits into SAP Cloud ERP

We showed demos of the latest user experience innovations in SAP Cloud ERP, also known as SAP S/4HANA Cloud. Starting with My Home, we explored enterprise search, card creation, smart situations, and AI-powered recommendations. In the SAP Fiori apps, we demonstrated proven productivity saving tools such as easy filter, easy fill, and smart summarization.

We briefly discussed SAP Fiori, including how it relates to design, development, and delivery of new apps in SAP Cloud ERP. This foundational session set the stage for the rest of the day, covering how SAP is bringing embedded AI to business users while keeping the SAP Design System and enterprise product standards at the center of the user experience.

Your data and queries are safe when using SAP AI.

There is often confusion when we talk about embedded AI and Joule. These two approaches are complementary. Joule provides a conversational interface to access SAP’s broad range of capabilities. It spans the entire autonomous suite.

Embedded AI provides targeted capabilities in both My Home and SAP Fiori elements apps within SAP Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition). People can use their existing apps and business processes while benefitting from the productivity enhancements that AI provides. Any embedded AI capabilities can also be accessed through Joule.

Partners focused on bringing AI to customers on any platform

The sponsors demonstrated a deep knowledge of the SAP market with everyone sharing different ways of bringing AI capabilities to customers, regardless of whether they were running on-prem or in the cloud. Here is a list of the partner sessions:

  • SAP UX Eras tour: from Dynpro modifications to clean core – what we learned and how to do it right in 2026 (Dassian)
  • Disappearing Ink: Use Agents to Automatically Eliminate Manual Data Entry Across Your Business (cbs – Corporate Business Solutions)
  • From On-Premise to the Future: Enabling Agentic AI Across SAP Landscapes (eXXcellent solutions)
  • Have Your Agent Call My Agent: 20 Joule Agents, Claude, and Reimagining of the Fiori Experience (Mindset Consulting)

Each session offered a different angle on the same broad question every SAP customer is asking right now: how do I bring AI and agents into my SAP landscape in a way that delivers measurable value? The variety of approaches sparked great discussion and gave attendees plenty of reasons to visit the partners during the breaks to go into more detail than the 20-minute talks allowed.

Successful AI implementation is about productivity – the hands-on workshops demonstrated this

The afternoon sessions we split into two blocks: one on application development and the other on new features.

In the application development talk, we talked about SAP Fiori elements, SAP Fiori tools, and building apps using clean core principles. We followed that up with a hands-on exercise where participants could build an app using our latest tools and have the chance to try vibe coding to add and change features in the app without coding. They commented that this was much faster than traditional methods for developing new custom apps.

SAP Fiori elements makes it easy to build apps with enterprise features.

 

The next section was on Smart Helpers, a new way that business users can automate routine tasks in SAP Fiori apps in their SAP Cloud ERP system. We discussed the reasons why we built this capability and encouraged people to think about areas in their business where they could streamline recurring tasks in SAP. Then, they had a chance to try out the capabilities on a few simple scenarios. Smart Helpers operate according to these principles:

  • The user is in charge
  • IT remains in control
  • They are built on SAP Fiori apps
  • No coding is required

People shared several scenarios they would consider automating with Smart Helpers.

Tour of the Experience Center highlighted broader SAP capabilities

To provide a broader look at SAP’s Autonomous Suite we scheduled a tour of the Experience Center in the AppHaus. Visitors followed fictitious company, S.Tech, to see how they transform complex operational and supply chain challenges into opportunities. People saw different SAP solutions working together in a manufacturing scenario and enjoyed seeing the new demos.

 

What’s next for UX innovation in SAP Cloud ERP?

The day closed with a sneak preview of new functionality around Smart Helpers and a deeper dive into Joule Work. This triggered a rich discussion on Joule Work and how it fits into SAP’s portfolio. We addressed the following questions:

  • Does Joule Work replace SAP Fiori?
    No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. 
  • What is the future of SAP Fiori apps?
    SAP Fiori apps will coexist with Joule Work for the foreseeable future, just as SAP GUI transactions coexist with SAP Fiori apps. We are continuing to build SAP Fiori apps for SAP Cloud ERP and enhance the tools that we use and you can use to develop your own SAP Fiori apps. Tooling will include increasing amounts of AI to reduce the amount of coding required to create new apps.
  • When will we see all the cool embedded AI innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition?
    We are working on moving most of the embedded AI capabilities to private cloud. Here is our current plan as of May 2026. As with any development plans or roadmaps, this is subject to change.
 

Public Beta

Public GA

Private GA

AI-assisted in My Home

   

AI-assisted Smart Personalization (Cards)

 

x

Planned

AI-assisted Smart Personalization (Apps and Tiles)
