Ob Greenfield-Implementierung bei der Medizinischen Universität Lausitz, S/4HANA-Migration bei Hapag-Lloyd oder KI-Rollout bei BASF: Ohne systematische Begleitung der Menschen bleibt jede Technologieinvestition unter ihrem Potenzial. Die oft zitierte These, dass zwei Drittel aller Change-Projekte scheitern, ist wissenschaftlich nicht belegt.  Die Erfolgswahrscheinlichkeit ist deutlich höher, wenn Change professionell und früh verankert wird.

Das zeigt, wie wichtig es ist, bereits vor Projektstart festzulegen, woran man den Erfolg von Change Management misst, bspw. von Werten in Pulsbefragungen, Führungskräfte-Engagement, oder Nutzungsraten im System. Die Medizinische Universität Lausitz hat Change als größte Einzelposition im SAP-Vertrag eingeplant mit 4,3 Prozent des Volumens und nennt es „Investitionssicherung". Wer Change erst kurz vor Go-Live beginnt, kann diese Investition später doppelt bezahlen.

Lokal entscheiden, global standardisieren

One-Size-Fits-All funktioniert nicht – das zeigte sich bei der Bundesbehörde ebenso wie beim Logistikkonzern. Hapag-Lloyd bringt das auf den Punkt: 265 Change Ambassadors in sechs Regionen, die in der Landessprache kommunizieren und als Multiplikatoren wirken. Das globale Programm setzt den Rahmen, aber die Transformation wird lokal vor Ort gewonnen. Auch Prof. Dr. Osterchrist bestätigt das wissenschaftlich: Mindestens 7 Prozent der Betroffenen müssen aktiv beteiligt sein und damit nicht nur informiert werden. Dialogformate schlagen One-Way-Kommunikation messbar.

Das zeigt, dass der Set-Up hinsichtlich Change Management die lokalen Gegebenheiten einer Organisation abbilden sollte. Regionale Change Leads und Multiplikatoren-Netzwerke sind empfehlenswert, da sie vor Ort in der Sprache ihrer Zielgruppen kommunizieren. Auch das Investment in Formate, die echte Mitentscheidung ermöglichen ist empfehlenswert – nicht nur Nutzung von Townhalls und Newsletter.

Lernen gehört in den Arbeits-Kontext, nicht in den Schulungsraum

Bei Präsenzschulungen multitasken viele der Teilnehmenden, fast jeder Dritte vergisst das Gelernte sehr schnell. Einmalige Schulungsereignisse kurz vor Go-Live ändern Verhalten nicht nachhaltig. MHP zeigte, dass Befähigung viel früher beginnen muss: Key User, Business Leads und Tester brauchen ein gemeinsames Verständnis des Projekts, bevor End-User-Trainings überhaupt greifen können. Die Telekom MMS verknüpft das ADKAR-Modell mit SAP WalkMe, um Lernen direkt im Arbeitsfluss zu verankern – Schritt-für-Schritt-Anleitungen, Tooltips und Prozessautomatisierung genau dort, wo Mitarbeitende gerade arbeiten. Und SAP selbst hat das Lernformat neu gedacht: NotebookLM im SAP Learning Hub, Podcasts, Mindmaps, interaktive KI-Gespräche basierend auf verifizierten Quellen, in über 80 Sprachen, als „Safe Space" für Fragen, die man sonst nicht stellen würde. Zertifizierungen testen nun zudem keine auswendig gelernten Antworten mehr, sondern Handlungskompetenz. An sich ist das Thema Handlungsrelevanz auch alles nichts neues, jedoch nun ist es vereinfacht durch KI und in der Breite angekommen.

KI braucht Governance und Führungskräfte, die vorangehen

KI ist kein Zukunftsthema mehr. Am KIT entstanden 31 didaktische Chatbots in sieben Tagen, oder Joule Studio ermöglicht es Business-Experten, in natürlicher Sprache funktionierende KI-Agenten zu bauen. Gleichzeitig zeigt das KIT, was passiert, wenn KI ohne Rahmen wächst: Schatten-KI, unkontrollierte Tool-Landschaften, Kostenexplosion. Die Antwort ist nicht Verbote, sondern eine strukturierte Ermöglichungsstrategie – zentrale Toolboxen, transparente Budgetierung, verpflichtende Basisqualifizierung, gemeinsames Arbeiten an konkreten Use-Cases. Dr. Laugwitz machte zudem deutlich, dass Governance kein Bremsklotz ist, sondern eine Voraussetzung für skalierbares Vertrauen mit Fairness, Transparenz und menschlicher Kontrolle.

Resümee und weiterführende Ressourcen

Das Forum war voll mit Inspiration, Ermutigung, Austausch. Eine Stimme einer Teilnehmerin war stellvertretend: „Als Changemanagerin fühle ich mich auf den Projekten oft sehr alleine mit dem Thema – weil es das erste ist, was gestrichen wird. Heute fand ich es wahnsinnig inspirierend, so viele Leute zu haben, die genauso für das Thema brennen. Das hat mich persönlich noch mal darin bestärkt, noch mehr dafür einzustehen." Geteiltes Leid ist manchmal weniger Leid, das Forum hat gezeigt, dass Gemeinschaft dabei keine Nebensache ist. Erste Reflektionen von Teilnehmenden gab es auf LinkedIn die ich hier verlinke: Hans Schmidt – Tobias Bartholome – Alexander Stehr – Christina Lehman – Simone Kunze – Katja Bachmann – Adelheid Speri-Praus – Andres Sexauer via KIT Account – Van Anh Vu – Jose Gamero – Selma Delija-Röhm

Hier ein graphical Recording des Tages - auch hier habe ich KI genutzt. 

Macht mit!

Die SAP Change & Training Community lebt vom Austausch. Wer eigene Erfahrungen teilen, von anderen lernen oder einfach mitreden möchte, ist herzlich eingeladen:

Monatliche Kaffee-Ecke: SAP Change & Training Kaffee-Ecke – Dialog, -  Praxisberichte, Co-Creation.

Kommt in die LinkedIn-Gruppe oder SAP Community Gruppe und macht dort gerne aktiv mit und bringt Euch ein.  

Podcast auf Apple & Spotify sowie der Newsletter dazu auf LinkedIn.

Weitere Ressourcen

  • Event-Agenda: SAP Learning and Adoption Forum 2026 Agenda
  • SAP Learning Hub: KI-gestütztes Lernen mit NotebookLM-Integration, Multiformat-Lernen, Live-Sessions – learning.sap.com
  • SAP AI Ethics: Policy, Handbook und Bewertungsprozess – sap.com/ai-ethics
  • SAP OCM Website
  • OCM Accelerators: Vorlagen und Best Practices im SAP Roadmap Viewer
  • Webinarreihe: „SAP Projekte – Der Mensch im Fokus" – Aufzeichnungen verfügbar
  • BASF AI & Enablement: Artificial Intelligence at BASF
  • KIT KI-Strategie: Diskussionspapier beim Hochschulforum Digitalisierung zur agentischen KI an Hochschulen
  • Kontakt SAP Learning & Change Team: training.mee@sap.com

Eure Stimme zählt

Welche Erfahrungen habt ihr mit Change Management und KI-Integration gemacht? Was funktioniert in euren Organisationen, was nicht? Welche Herausforderung betrifft euch am meisten, und wie könnte die Community unterstützen?

Schreibt es in die Kommentare.

entry.title: World Cup 2026 Predictions with SAP RPT and AI Agents
entry.content:
Football has been a personal obsession since 2002, when a seven-year-old version of me watched Brazil lift the World Cup and decided this sport was basically magic. Twenty-four years later, the obsession hasn't changed — but my toolbox has. A few months ago I started working with SAP's Relational Pretrained Transformer (RPT) on enterprise prediction problems and one question wouldn't leave me alone: could this thing predict football?

 it can, within the limits of honest machine learning. This post walks through what I built, what I learned, and why one of the most useful things the system does is admit when it doesn't know.
 
The Problem With Football Predictions (and Enterprise Data)
Traditional football prediction has the same shape as a lot of enterprise analytics: the signal is scattered across multiple sources (FIFA rankings, historical results, form data, injury news), the volume is manageable, but the synthesis is slow and manual. A pundit watches twelve matches and synthesises an opinion over a weekend. An enterprise analyst runs a spreadsheet for three days. Neither has time to be wrong gracefully.
The interesting thing about SAP's RPT model is that it's designed for exactly this situation. Not "you have ten million rows and six months to train a model," but "you have a few hundred historical rows, the problem changes faster than your retraining schedule, and you need a prediction now." Football match history — one World Cup every four years — is precisely this kind of sparse, high-stakes dataset.
The key idea: RPT is an in-context learner for tabular data. You don't train it on your problem — you hand it a set of historical rows and ask it to reason from them directly at inference time. Think of it as the world's most structured K-NN that actually read the footnotes. No gradient descent. No MLflow experiment to babysit. No retraining cycle after Brazil's last-minute squad change. You swap the context rows, and the model adapts instantly.
For comparison: XGBoost would be the better choice if you had thousands of labelled rows and a data engineer with free time. For sparse, fast-moving data in an SAP ecosystem — where you want predictions inside the trust boundary, sharing the same OAuth, resource groups, and audit trail as the rest of your AI Core workloads — RPT earns its keep.
 
The Architecture: Two Scouts, One Verdict


Framework used:
Python
FastAPI
Langchain

SAP AI Core - GenAI Hub:

sap-rpt-1-smallTabular ML prediction — match outcome, goals, confidence
openai-gpt-5.4LLM for data-agent reasoning and final synthesis
Perplexity sonar-proReal-time news — injuries, suspensions, form

 

 
The system has three layers, and the design philosophy is straightforward: separate fast facts from slow context, and make the fast path resilient when the slow path fails.

At the core sits an RPT agent that collects structured match statistics — FIFA rankings, goals averages, form scores, head-to-head records — and calls the RPT model on SAP AI Core. This is the fast path. It returns a match outcome (W/L/D), goals estimate, and a confidence score per target in a few seconds.

Running in parallel on a separate Python thread is a news agent that calls a research LLM (via the SAP AI Core orchestration deployment) to find injuries, suspensions, and pre-match psychology. This is the enrichment path. It's valuable, but it's also occasionally slow — like the pundit who went to check social media for injury news and came back fifteen minutes later with strong opinions and no sources. So it runs with a hard 120-second timeout. If it finishes in time, great. If not, the prediction completes on RPT alone and the final verdict explicitly says "no news context available."

The final layer is a synthesis LLM call that reads both results and writes a human-readable verdict: SCORE / CONFIDENCE / REASONS. Three lines. Clear enough for a football fan, honest enough for an engineer.

What's deliberately not in this architecture: a LangGraph agent loop for orchestration. An early version used one — until I noticed I was burning one entire LLM round-trip just to decide which of two deterministic tasks to run next. Python's `ThreadPoolExecutor` does the same job faster and gives you hard control over the news timeout. Sometimes the right tool is a ten-line concurrent.futures block, not an agentic reasoning loop.
 
The Data: One Match, One Row, No Exceptions
The context rows come from StatsBomb open data — publicly available match statistics covering FIFA World Cups and major international tournaments. Each historical match becomes one row, always from a single team's perspective.

That "single perspective" rule tripped me up early. If you include both "Brazil vs France (Brazil perspective)" and "France vs Brazil (France perspective)" from the same game, you've added noise, not information. The feature vectors are mirror images. RPT's similarity search pulls in the same game twice, double-weights it, and produces inconsistent context. One match, one row — enforced by a pre-flight check that returns a descriptive error back to the agent for self-correction before anything reaches the API.

For teams not covered by StatsBomb (smaller federations, newer sides), the data agent estimates values from FIFA ranking history and flags those rows explicitly in the output. You'll see this in the demo result below — and the model's honesty about its own data quality turned out to be one of the most useful parts of the whole system.
 
The SAP AI Core Deployment
Before the demo, a quick note on a routing issue that cost me an afternoon and might save you one.

SAP AI Core distinguishes between two deployment types: foundation-models and orchestration. LiteLLM's sap/ model prefix talks exclusively to orchestration deployments — it calls /lm/deployments` and filters for scenarioId == "orchestration". If your deployment is foundation-models, the filtered list comes back empty and you get a cryptic list index out of range deep inside LiteLLM's transformation module. Not the error message you'd write if you were trying to help someone.

The fix is to switch to the openai/ prefix with your deployment URL set as api_base. LiteLLM then routes through its OpenAI-compatible handler instead, which is perfectly happy talking to either deployment type. It's essentially the airport gate analogy: you got on the right plane, but you were standing at the wrong gate and the gate agent wasn't going to tell you where the plane actually was.

Check your deployment's scenarioId in SAP AI Launchpad before choosing your prefix. It's a two-minute check that prevents a two-hour debug session.
 
What It Actually Looks Like
======================================================================
FINAL PREDICTION — Germany vs Ivory Coast
======================================================================
SCORE : Germany 5 - 1 Ivory Coast
CONFIDENCE : HIGH
REASONS :
- RPT model output favors Germany to win 5-1 with 67% confidence, driven by a stronger baseline ranking (16 vs 49), higher recent goals scored average (2.2 vs 1.2), and better defensive record (0.8 conceded vs 1.4).
- Key player impact: Germany have no confirmed absences, while Ivory Coast’s main squad concern is Evan Ndicka’s hamstring issue after missing the opener; Germany also bring major attacking threats in Deniz Undav, Florian Wirtz, and Jamal Musiala, while Amad Diallo is Ivory Coast’s main danger after scoring the winner vs Ecuador.
- Head-to-head and form context: the only listed meeting was a 2-2 draw in 2009, but current form strongly favors Germany, who are on a five-match winning run and coming off a 7-1 win, compared with Ivory Coast’s narrower 1-0 victory and W-W-L-W-W recent sequence.
- Any other deciding factor: Germany’s opener showed explosive attacking momentum, and Ivory Coast’s possible defensive disruption if Ndicka is unavailable supports the model’s expectation of a comfortable Germany win, despite the RPT reliability caveat about an internal opponent-label error.
TIME TAKEN : 154.0s
Finished : 2026-06-17 19:38:16
======================================================================
 
What This Means Beyond Football

The football scenario is deliberately concrete, but the architecture pattern generalises directly to enterprise use cases:

  • Maintenance order triage: RPT predicts criticality based on historical order patterns; an LLM agent pulls recent incident notes; synthesis produces a priority score with reasons.
  • Invoice exception handling: structured approval history as RPT context; free-text comments as LLM enrichment; parallel execution with a timeout on the enrichment path.
  • Sales forecasting: tabular history through RPT; market news and product launch context through a research model; same CONFIDENCE: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW output pattern.

The common thread is sparse, fast-moving tabular data paired with unstructured context — which describes a substantial fraction of what SAP customers actually build on BTP.

 
What I'd Do Differently

A few things I'd change with hindsight:

Schema versioning from day one. The RPT COLUMNS list is the contract between the data agent and the model. The first time I added a feature, I had to regenerate the entire context dataset. Treat it like a database migration, not a Python constant.

 
Final Thoughts

The model's confidence on Brazil vs France came in at 87% — which means there is a 13% chance of the kind of footballing chaos that makes this sport worth watching in the first place. I have made my peace with this.

What I didn't expect was how much of the project's value would come from the parts designed to handle failure well: the news timeout, the data quality flags, the explicit Medium confidence. In production systems, graceful degradation isn't a nice-to-have. It's what determines whether your users trust the output at all.

If you're working on similar problems — tabular prediction on sparse enterprise data, or parallel agent patterns on SAP AI Core — I'd love to hear what you're building. Comments are open, and I promise to respond faster than a 120-second timeout.

The technical stuffs

Learn more about the RPT - https://learning.sap.com/courses/genai-lpad-gnd/bt-sap-rpt-1.
1. Enable the RPT model from SAP AI launchpad 


2. Sample RPT code - 
sample data - 
{ "prediction_config": { "target_columns": [ { "name": "match_outcome", "prediction_placeholder": null, "task_type": "classification" }, { "name": "goals", "prediction_placeholder": null, "task_type": "regression" }, { "name": "vs_goals", "prediction_placeholder": null, "task_type": "regression" } ] }, "index_column": "__row_idx__", "parse_data_types": true, "data_schema": { "__row_idx__": { "dtype": "numeric" }, "ranking": { "dtype": "numeric" }, "goals_scored_avg": { "dtype": "numeric" }, "form_score": { "dtype": "numeric" }, "match_outcome": { "dtype": "string" }, "goals": { "dtype": "numeric" }, "vs_goals": { "dtype": "numeric" } }, "rows": [ { "__row_idx__": 0, "ranking": 3, "goals_scored_avg": 2.1, "form_score": 8, "match_outcome": "W", "goals": 2, "vs_goals": 0 }, { "__row_idx__": 1, "ranking": 10, "goals_scored_avg": 1.4, "form_score": 5, "match_outcome": "L", "goals": 1, "vs_goals": 2 }, { "__row_idx__": 2, "ranking": 6, "goals_scored_avg": 1.9, "form_score": 7, "match_outcome": "W", "goals": 3, "vs_goals": 1 }, { "__row_idx__": 3, "ranking": 12, "goals_scored_avg": 1.3, "form_score": 4, "match_outcome": "L", "goals": 0, "vs_goals": 1 }, { "__row_idx__": 4, "ranking": 8, "goals_scored_avg": 1.7, "form_score": 6, "match_outcome": "D", "goals": 1, "vs_goals": 1 }, { "__row_idx__": 5, "ranking": 2, "goals_scored_avg": 2.4, "form_score": 9, "match_outcome": "W", "goals": 3, "vs_goals": 0 }, { "__row_idx__": 6, "ranking": 15, "goals_scored_avg": 1.2, "form_score": 3, "match_outcome": "L", "goals": 1, "vs_goals": 3 }, { "__row_idx__": 7, "ranking": 9, "goals_scored_avg": 1.6, "form_score": 6, "match_outcome": "W", "goals": 2, "vs_goals": 1 }, { "__row_idx__": 8, "ranking": 5, "goals_scored_avg": 1.8, "form_score": 7, "match_outcome": null, "goals": null, "vs_goals": null } ] }


python code - 

import os
import json
from pathlib import Path
import requests
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

token_resp = requests.post(
f"{os.environ['AICORE_AUTH_URL']}/oauth/token",
data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
auth=(os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"], os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"]),
timeout=30,
)
token_resp.raise_for_status()
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

payload_path = Path(__file__).with_name("test_data_rpt.json")
with payload_path.open("r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
payload = json.load(f)

url = os.environ["AICORE_RPT_DEPLOYMENT_URL"] + "/predict"
resource_group = os.getenv("AICORE_RPT_RESOURCE_GROUP") or os.getenv("AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP", "default")
headers = {
"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
"AI-Resource-Group": resource_group,
"Content-Type": "application/json",
}

resp = requests.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers, timeout=60)
resp.raise_for_status()
print(resp.json())​

 

3. LangGraph Checkpointers - click here to learn more about checkpointing. 

import logging
import os
import sqlite3

from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import InMemorySaver
from langgraph.checkpoint.sqlite import SqliteSaver

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

MEMORY_DIR = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), ".memory")
_DB_PATH = os.path.join(MEMORY_DIR, "checkpoints.db")

""" Runtime checkpointer (in-process singleton)"""

_runtime: InMemorySaver | None = None


def get_runtime_checkpointer() -> InMemorySaver:
"""Return the shared in-process InMemorySaver (created once per session)."""
global _runtime
if _runtime is None:
_runtime = InMemorySaver()
logger.debug("[memory] runtime checkpointer initialised")
return _runtime


"""Persistent checkpointer (SQLite, survives restarts) """

def get_persistent_checkpointer() -> SqliteSaver:
"""Return a SqliteSaver backed by a2a_agents/.memory/checkpoints.db."""
os.makedirs(MEMORY_DIR, exist_ok=True)
conn = sqlite3.connect(_DB_PATH, check_same_thread=False)
logger.debug("[memory] persistent checkpointer opened: %s", _DB_PATH)
return SqliteSaver(conn)

 

 Feel free to post a comment on your thoughts.

entry.title: How I Used Microsoft Copilot and Azure Services to Improve SAP S/4HANA Project Tracking
entry.content:
How I Used Microsoft Copilot and Azure Services to Improve SAP S/4HANA Project Tracking and Governance

Introduction

One of the biggest challenges in large SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is not the implementation itself—it is maintaining visibility across hundreds of project activities, risks, defects, decisions, and action items spread across multiple teams and systems.

During global SAP transformation programs, project information is often distributed across several platforms:

  • Azure DevOps for defects and sprint activities

  • Microsoft Project for schedules

  • ServiceNow for incidents and change requests

  • Microsoft Teams for collaboration

  • SharePoint for project documentation

  • SAP Solution Manager or Cloud ALM for project execution activities

As project managers, we spend significant time consolidating information from these sources before we can make informed decisions.

Recently, I explored how Microsoft Copilot combined with Azure services can help project managers improve governance, risk management, reporting, and executive visibility throughout an SAP transformation lifecycle.

The Traditional Project Tracking Challenge

In a typical SAP S/4HANA transformation, a weekly status review often requires collecting information from multiple workstreams:

  • Program Management Office

  • Data Migration Team

  • Integration Team

  • Testing Team

  • Security Team

  • Change Management Team

  • Business Process Owners

The challenge is that critical project information exists in multiple repositories.

Typical Information Landscape

Azure DevOps
   │
   ├── Defects
   ├── User Stories
   └── Sprint Progress

Microsoft Project
   │
   ├── Project Schedule
   ├── Milestones
   └── Dependencies

ServiceNow
   │
   ├── Incidents
   ├── Changes
   └── Service Requests

Microsoft Teams
   │
   ├── Meetings
   ├── Discussions
   └── Action Items

SharePoint
   │
   ├── Documents
   ├── RAID Logs
   └── Status Reports

As a result, project managers spend considerable effort gathering information instead of analyzing risks and making decisions.

Introducing Microsoft Copilot into SAP Project Governance

Microsoft Copilot provides an opportunity to transform project management activities from manual reporting to intelligent project governance.

Rather than searching through multiple systems, project leaders can ask questions in natural language such as:

  • Which workstreams are currently behind schedule?

  • What are the highest-priority risks this week?

  • Which defects remain open beyond SLA?

  • What actions were assigned during steering committee meetings?

  • Which milestones are at risk for the upcoming release?

Instead of manually preparing reports, Copilot can help summarize information from connected project repositories.

Architecture Overview

The following conceptual architecture demonstrates how Azure services and Copilot can support SAP project tracking.

Architecture Diagram

+---------------------------------------------------+
|              SAP Transformation Program           |
+---------------------------------------------------+

          Data Sources
-----------------------------------------------------

 Azure DevOps      Microsoft Project
      |                    |
      |                    |
      +----------+---------+
                 |
                 |
 ServiceNow   SharePoint   Teams
      |           |          |
      +-----------+----------+
                  |
                  v

+--------------------------------------+
|      Microsoft Azure Services        |
+--------------------------------------+
| Azure Data Factory                   |
| Azure Logic Apps                     |
| Azure AI Services                    |
| Azure Storage                        |
| Azure Data Lake                      |
+--------------------------------------+
                  |
                  v

+--------------------------------------+
|         Microsoft Copilot            |
+--------------------------------------+
| Risk Summarization                   |
| Defect Trend Analysis                |
| Executive Status Reporting           |
| Action Item Tracking                 |
| Meeting Summaries                    |
+--------------------------------------+
                  |
                  v

+--------------------------------------+
| SAP Program Leadership Dashboard     |
+--------------------------------------+
| PMO                                  |
| Steering Committee                   |
| Business Stakeholders                |
| Program Leadership                   |
+--------------------------------------+

Use Case 1: Automated Executive Status Reporting

One of the most time-consuming activities for project managers is preparing executive status reports.

Traditionally, project managers gather information from multiple teams and manually create PowerPoint presentations.

With Copilot, project leaders can generate summaries based on:

  • Open risks

  • Defect trends

  • Milestone completion

  • Change requests

  • Resource constraints

Example prompt:

"Summarize all high-priority risks, critical defects, and delayed milestones for the SAP S/4HANA transformation program during the past seven days."

This significantly reduces manual reporting effort and allows project managers to focus on decision-making rather than document preparation.

Use Case 2: Risk Monitoring and Early Warning Indicators

Risk management is critical during SAP transformation programs.

In one project, recurring defects appeared during integration testing across multiple regions. Individually, each defect seemed manageable, but collectively they indicated a broader data quality issue.

Using AI-assisted analysis, project teams can identify:

  • Recurring defect patterns

  • Escalating risks

  • Delayed action items

  • Resource bottlenecks

  • Testing readiness concerns

Example Risk Analysis Flow

Defect Data
      +
Meeting Notes
      +
Action Items
      +
Schedule Updates
      |
      v

Azure AI Analysis

      |
      v

Risk Pattern Detection

      |
      v

Copilot Summary

      |
      v

Project Leadership Alert

This approach helps identify potential problems before they impact major milestones.

Use Case 3: Meeting Action Tracking

A common challenge in SAP programs is following up on action items generated during workshops and governance meetings.

Copilot can help by:

  • Summarizing meeting discussions

  • Extracting action items

  • Identifying owners

  • Tracking due dates

  • Highlighting overdue actions

Instead of manually reviewing meeting notes, project managers receive a consolidated action dashboard.

Use Case 4: Defect Trend Analysis

During testing cycles, hundreds or even thousands of defects may be logged.

Copilot can assist by identifying:

  • Defect hotspots

  • Repeated root causes

  • Process areas generating the highest defect volume

  • Regional implementation concerns

This helps testing managers prioritize corrective actions more effectively.

Lessons Learned

Through my experience managing enterprise transformation programs, I have learned that AI does not replace project managers.

Instead, it enhances their ability to:

  • Process information faster

  • Identify risks earlier

  • Improve governance visibility

  • Reduce manual reporting effort

  • Focus on strategic decision-making

The most successful approach combines:

  • Human expertise

  • Business process knowledge

  • Governance discipline

  • AI-assisted insights

Conclusion

The future of SAP project management is not about replacing project managers with AI. It is about empowering project leaders with better visibility and faster access to information.

By combining Microsoft Copilot with Azure services, organizations can transform project tracking from a manual reporting exercise into a proactive governance capability.

For SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, this means improved transparency, faster decision-making, better risk management, and ultimately a higher probability of successful program delivery.

As SAP transformations continue to grow in complexity, AI-powered project governance will become an increasingly important capability for program leaders seeking to deliver successful business outcomes.

entry.title: The Missing Layer in Every SAP Go-Live: Why Hypercare Should Be Built Before Go-Live, Not After!
entry.content:
The fifth time I watched a warehouse operative stand frozen in front of a red error screen at 6 AM on go-live day — waiting for a support consultant who wasn't there — I stopped thinking about the symptom and started thinking about the cause.

I have lived this exact scene across five SAP EWM implementations — frozen food, automotive, pharmaceutical, building products, tires. Different industries, different warehouses, different error messages. The same scene every time. A worker who was trained three weeks ago, on a process that has slightly changed since, hits an error the training never covered. The shift supervisor doesn't know the answer. The consultant who configured that exact process rotated off the project last week, or is asleep in a different time zone, or is on another call. The truck is waiting at the dock. And the person who is supposed to keep goods moving is standing still, holding an RF gun, looking at a screen they don't understand.

This is hypercare. And after living it five times, I've concluded we've been solving it the wrong way.

Why Hypercare Keeps Failing — Structurally

The standard answer to a rough go-live is "throw more consultants at it." More floor support. Longer hypercare window. Round-the-clock coverage. I no longer believe that's the answer, because the failures aren't random — they're structural. Six causes show up on every project:

Consultant rotation. The person who configured your wave logic, your POSC/LOSC routing, your putaway strategy, your PPF output — they are not on the floor at 6 AM on day one. The knowledge that would resolve the error in thirty seconds has already walked out the door.

The 24/7 coverage gap. Warehouses run three shifts. Hypercare staffing rarely does. The night shift hits the same errors as the day shift, with even less support.

Accent and language barriers. A global implementation means the support consultant and the floor worker often don't share a first language. Under time pressure, on a noisy dock, that gap becomes a wall.

Knowledge walkout. When hypercare ends, the consultants leave — and every hard-won resolution they discovered leaves with them. The next project rediscovers the same errors from scratch.

The same errors, every project. Across five implementations I have watched teams independently rediscover the identical handful of go-live errors — a missing PPF condition, a queue stuck in /SCWM/MON, an exception code nobody assigned. We never built an institutional memory.

The screenshot barrier. When a worker can't describe an error in words, they photograph it. That photo gets forwarded to a consultant, who interprets it, who replies. The whole support loop already runs on screenshots — we just haven't built anything to receive them.

More Consultants Is Not the Answer

Look at those six causes together and a pattern emerges: this is not a headcount problem. It is a knowledge continuity and availability problem.

Adding more junior, rotating consultants doesn't fix continuity — it amplifies the churn. What's actually missing is something that holds the project-specific knowledge, is available on every shift, speaks the worker's language, accepts a screenshot, and never rotates off.

That isn't a person. It's a system — and it should be the floor's first-level support. Not a replacement for the consultant, but the layer that sits in front of them: it answers the 70-80% of go-live questions that are known, repetitive, and project-specific, and escalates only the genuinely new problems to a human. The consultant's scarce time stops being spent re-answering the same wave error twenty times a shift, and starts being spent on the issues that actually need an architect.

Here's the part that I think is genuinely new.

An Embedded AI Agent — Built in Parallel With Testing

The concept is an AI hypercare agent embedded in the implementation. Plenty of people have proposed an "AI chatbot for SAP support," so let me be precise about the design principles that make this different — because the difference is the whole point.

1. Built in parallel with testing — not after UAT. This is the headline. Every AI support tool I have seen is deployed after go-live, then slowly trained on real incidents while real people suffer. This agent is trained during the testing phase — every SIT defect, every UAT resolution, every project-specific configuration quirk feeds it as the project happens. It arrives at go-live already 90% ready, not as a blank slate that learns while the warehouse bleeds.

2. Solution-Architect-curated, weekly. A human quality gate sits in the knowledge pipeline. Each week during testing, the SA reviews what the agent has ingested and validates it. This is what stops the noise problem that makes generic chatbots useless — the agent learns verified resolutions, not every wrong guess from a test cycle.

3. Multimodal — screenshot first. The primary interface is the photograph of the error screen, because that is already how the floor communicates. The worker shows the agent what they see, exactly as they would show a consultant.

4. A non-negotiable contracted deliverable. This is a commercial reframing, not just a technical one — and I'll come back to it, because it's the part that makes the whole thing actually happen.

5. Self-learning with a validation gate. Resolved tickets feed back into the knowledge base — but only after a Solution Architect signs off, never automatically. This principle matters enough that I give it its own section below, because it is what separates this from a chatbot that confidently repeats wrong answers.

6. Built for the human factors. Accent-independent, anxiety-aware, available to a worker who was rotated onto this line yesterday. These aren't technical features. They're the reason the gap exists, and only someone who has watched it five times would design for them.

7. A controlled, always-current knowledge surface. Beyond resolving errors, the bot is trained on the project's own documentation library — OPLs, work instructions, process guides — and returns only the latest approved version of any document. It becomes the single place the floor can ask, in plain English, "what do I do when X happens?" and trust the answer is current.

The Bot Is a System Object — So It Gets Signed Off Like One

Here's a principle I'd argue is non-negotiable: on go-live day, this bot cannot be a surprise.

In a new SAP S/4HANA embedded EWM deployment, no warehouse process reaches production without passing through the full validation chain — unit test, SIT, UAT, regression, cutover sign-off — approved by the relevant process owners. A wave release strategy is signed off by the outbound lead. A production staging flow is signed off by the manufacturing and warehouse leads. An inbound GR process is signed off by the receiving lead.

The hypercare bot must be treated as exactly that kind of object. It moves through the same gates as every warehouse process:

  • SIT — the bot's resolutions are validated against the same test scripts the processes are validated against. If a resolution it gives would fail the SIT script, it doesn't ship.
  • UAT — the actual business users — the shipping clerk, the production supervisor, the receiving specialist — interact with the bot during UAT, not for the first time on go-live morning. They sign off that its answers match how they will actually work.
  • Regression and cutover — the bot's knowledge base is version-controlled and frozen at cutover alongside the configuration, with the same change-control discipline.

And critically: the bot is trained and socialized by the Organizational Change Management (OCM) team, not dropped in by IT. The same OCM workstream that runs the training, builds the work instructions, and manages the floor's readiness is the workstream that introduces the bot. So when the shipping clerk, the production supervisor, or the inbound receiving specialist opens it at 5 AM on day one, it is a familiar tool they were trained on and helped shape during UAT — not a random, fancy, surprise bot that nobody trusts. Trust is the entire game in hypercare. A tool the floor met for the first time on go-live day has zero trust. A tool they helped validate for six weeks has earned it.

What It Actually Looks Like — Four Real Scenarios

Let me make it concrete across the warehouse flows, in an embedded S/4HANA 2025 EWM deployment. These are representative of the exact class of issues that consume hypercare on every project — and each one shows a different capability the bot needs.

Outbound — the shipping coordinator and the "stock is there, but it isn't" problem. The coordinator runs the wave simulator in the shipping cockpit and it fails — the wave won't simulate. They check the warehouse monitor and the stock shows as F2, unrestricted-use in the warehouse, so by every signal on their screen the stock is available. The real cause is upstream: the batch is restricted because the QM team never released it, and the standard simulator doesn't surface that as the reason it failed. This is a genuine product gap — the simulator tells you it failed, not that a QM batch status is the cause. The bot closes that gap. The coordinator describes the failure in plain English; the bot, trained on this project's QM-EWM batch-release interplay, immediately points to the batch's QM status as the thing to check rather than the warehouse stock type — turning a forty-minute "but the stock is right there" investigation into a thirty-second answer. The same finding is also logged as a candidate enhancement to the simulator itself.

Outbound — the one-off scenario and the right version of the right document. A truck comes back to the yard because it was overloaded — a rare exception the coordinator handles maybe twice a year and has no memorized procedure for. There is an OPL for it, sitting in a shared drive among hundreds of other documents, and quite possibly in three slightly different versions. The bot was trained to reference that document library and tested to return only the latest approved version of any requested procedure. The coordinator simply asks, in plain English, what to do when a loaded truck returns — and gets the current OPL, not a superseded one, without hunting through folders or pinging a consultant to find out which version is live. This is the bot as a controlled, always-current knowledge surface over documentation that already exists but nobody can find under pressure.

Inbound — the receiving specialist who packed it wrong and needs to start over. The specialist realizes they packed an inbound delivery incorrectly on the first pass and needs to repack. The bot walks them through the recovery step by step: delete the existing handling units, use the Change Inbound Deliveries Fiori app to repack cleanly, then collect the new HU labels that print automatically from the assigned printer. This is the bot as a patient, step-by-step floor guide for a correction the specialist was never specifically trained on. And here is the important part — the bot knows the boundary of its own competence. If the new label does not come out of the Zebra printer automatically, and the bot was not designed or tested to troubleshoot printer output, it does not guess. It raises a ticket at the appropriate priority, assigns it to the right team, and tells the specialist it has done so. The specialist isn't left re-framing the same question to a hypercare consultant three times — the escalation happens cleanly and the resolution pace improves.

Production — the supervisor whose stock won't appear in MES. The production supervisor cannot see stock in their MES that should have synced from the EWM monitor. This is one of the most common and most painful go-live issues in a manufacturing landscape — an integration failure across the ERP–MES–EWM chain, where a posting in one system doesn't propagate to the next. The bot is trained to do exactly what a consultant would do to triage it: it takes a screenshot and/or the relevant data, optionally a line or two of context, and works through the integration checkpoints — did the material document post, did the EWM stock update, did the message reach MES — to isolate where the chain broke. If it can resolve it, it does. If it can't fully resolve it, the request routes to a real person already engaged on the project, with the triage already done.

The Bot Learns Only When a Trusted Human Signs Off

Those last two scenarios carry the most important design principle in the whole concept, so I want to state it plainly.

When a consultant resolves an escalated ticket, they cannot simply comment "issue fixed" and close it. That is how knowledge gets lost today. For the bot, a resolution only enters its knowledge base when a Solution Architect — or a peer of equivalent standing and trajectory — signs off that the resolution is correct, complete, and safe for the bot to repeat. Then the bot is retested against it, exactly as a configuration change would be retested.

This is the validation gate that separates this concept from a chatbot that confidently amplifies wrong answers. The bot relearns continuously — but only verified, signed-off knowledge, never an unreviewed "fixed" comment. A wrong resolution learned at machine speed and repeated to every shift is far more dangerous than no resolution at all. The human sign-off is not bureaucracy. It is the safety mechanism that makes self-learning trustworthy.

In all four scenarios: the error never had to be described in a second language, the consultant who built the config didn't have to be awake, the boundary of the bot's competence was respected, and every genuinely new resolution was captured — under human sign-off — for the next worker who hits it.

On the Architecture — Briefly

I'm keeping the stack high-level on purpose, because the originality here is when you build the agent and who curates it, not the technology. At a glance: a vision-capable LLM layer for the screenshot interpretation; a vector knowledge store trained on the project's own SIT/UAT defects, resolutions, and configuration documentation; integration into the implementation's ticketing system (the standard enterprise ITSM tool) so resolved incidents feed back through the SA validation gate; and a floor-friendly channel the workers already use. In an embedded EWM landscape it reads the same error and application-log surfaces the consultants already read — the difference is that it reads them at 5 AM, in every language, for every shift, and never forgets a resolution. The architecture is the easy part. The discipline of building and validating it alongside the warehouse processes is the hard part — and the valuable one.

Make It Non-Negotiable

Here's why this stays a concept unless the commercial model changes: as long as an AI hypercare agent is an optional add-on, it gets value-engineered out of the SOW on every budget-constrained project — which is all of them.

So don't make it optional. Price it into the implementation as a contracted hypercare deliverable, the same way you'd price the cutover plan or the training material. The economics are not subtle: the build cost of the agent is less than two weeks of a single floor consultant's hypercare time. One avoided week of go-live chaos pays for it. The argument to the client isn't "buy our AI" — it's "your hypercare will cost less and work better, and here's the line item."

That single reframing — from add-on to contracted deliverable — is what turns this from a nice idea into something that ships.

The Tool Does Not Yet Exist. That Is the Point.

I want to be clear and honest: I have not built this. What I have is five go-lives of watching the same preventable failure, a design that addresses each of its structural causes, and the conviction that the field is about to need it.

Every meaningful innovation in SAP consulting started as a named concept before it became a product. So I'm naming this one. An AI hypercare agent — built in parallel with testing, SA-curated, signed off through SIT and UAT like any warehouse process, socialized by OCM so the floor already trusts it, screenshot-first, and contracted as a deliverable — is, as far as I can find across SAP Community, SAP's roadmap, and the major SI tooling, not something anyone has yet put together this way.

I'd genuinely like to be challenged on that. If you've seen this built, tell me where — I want to learn from it. If you've lived the 6 AM frozen-screen scene too, tell me what you would add to the design. And if you're a Solution Architect or a delivery lead reading this before your next go-live: ask whether your hypercare plan is a stack of rotating consultants and hope, or something that actually holds the knowledge and stands in front of your floor as first-level support from hour one.

The scene I described plays out on warehouse floors every single week. We have the tools to end it. We've just been building them at the wrong time.

Yogesh Agarwal is an Enterprise Logistics Execution Specialist with 15+ years across SAP EWM and TM implementations in food, automotive, pharmaceutical, building products, and industrial sectors. He writes about SAP supply chain execution on SAP Community and LinkedIn.

entry.title: Multi-Agent LLM Pipeline with SAP RPT and other SAP AI Core LLMs for World Cup 2026 Match Prediction
entry.content:
Football has been a personal obsession since 2002, when a seven-year-old version of me watched Brazil lift the World Cup and decided this sport was basically magic. Twenty-four years later, the obsession hasn't changed but my toolbox has. A few months ago I started working with SAP's Relational Pretrained Transformer (RPT) on enterprise prediction problems and one question wouldn't leave me alone: could this thing predict football?
Spoiler
Spoiler: it can, within the limits of honest machine learning. This post walks through what I built, what I learned, and why one of the most useful things the system does is admit when it doesn't know.
The Problem With Football Predictions (and Enterprise Data)
 
Traditional football prediction has the same shape as a lot of enterprise analytics: the signal is scattered across multiple sources (FIFA rankings, historical results, form data, injury news), the volume is manageable, but the synthesis is slow and manual. A pundit watches twelve matches and synthesises an opinion over a weekend. An enterprise analyst runs a spreadsheet for three days. Neither has time to be wrong gracefully.

The interesting thing about SAP's RPT model is that it's designed for exactly this situation. Not "you have ten million rows and six months to train a model," but "you have a few hundred historical rows, the problem changes faster than your retraining schedule, and you need a prediction now." Football match history one World Cup every four years is precisely this kind of sparse, high-stakes dataset.

The key idea: RPT is an in-context learner for tabular data. You don't train it on your problem you hand it a set of historical rows and ask it to reason from them directly at inference time. Think of it as the world's most structured K-NN that actually read the footnotes. No gradient descent. No MLflow experiment to babysit. No retraining cycle after Brazil's last-minute squad change. You swap the context rows, and the model adapts instantly.

For comparison: XGBoost would be the better choice if you had thousands of labelled rows and a data engineer with free time. For sparse, fast-moving data in an SAP ecosystem where you want predictions inside the trust boundary, sharing the same OAuth, resource groups, and audit trail as the rest of your AI Core workloads RPT earns its keep.
 
The Architecture: Two Scouts, One Verdict
The system has three layers, and the design philosophy is straightforward: **separate fast facts from slow context, and make the fast path resilient when the slow path fails.**

At the core sits an RPT agent that collects structured match statistics FIFA rankings, goals averages, form scores, head-to-head records and calls the RPT model on SAP AI Core. This is the fast path. It returns a match outcome (W/L/D), goals estimate, and a confidence score per target in a few seconds.

Running in parallel on a separate Python thread is a news agent that calls a research LLM (via the SAP AI Core orchestration deployment) to find injuries, suspensions, and pre-match psychology. This is the enrichment path. It's valuable, but it's also occasionally slow like the pundit who went to check social media for injury news and came back fifteen minutes later with strong opinions and no sources. So it runs with a hard 120-second timeout. If it finishes in time, great. If not, the prediction completes on RPT alone and the final verdict explicitly says "no news context available."

The final layer is a synthesis LLM call that reads both results and writes a human-readable verdict: `SCORE / CONFIDENCE / REASONS`. Three lines. Clear enough for a football fan, honest enough for an engineer.

What's deliberately not in this architecture: a LangGraph agent loop for orchestration. An early version used one until I noticed I was burning one entire LLM round-trip just to decide which of two deterministic tasks to run next. Python's `ThreadPoolExecutor` does the same job faster and gives you hard control over the news timeout. Sometimes the right tool is a ten-line concurrent.futures block, not an agentic reasoning loop.
 
The Data: One Match, One Row, No Exceptions
The context rows come from StatsBomb open data publicly available match statistics covering FIFA World Cups and major international tournaments. Each historical match becomes one row, always from a single team's perspective.

That "single perspective" rule tripped me up early. If you include both "Brazil vs France (Brazil perspective)" and "France vs Brazil (France perspective)" from the same game, you've added noise, not information. The feature vectors are mirror images. RPT's similarity search pulls in the same game twice, double-weights it, and produces inconsistent context. One match, one row enforced by a pre-flight check that returns a descriptive error back to the agent for self-correction before anything reaches the API.

For teams not covered by StatsBomb (smaller federations, newer sides), the data agent estimates values from FIFA ranking history and flags those rows explicitly in the output. You'll see this in the demo result below and the model's honesty about its own data quality turned out to be one of the most useful parts of the whole system.
 
The SAP AI Core Deployment Gotcha
Before the demo, a quick note on a routing issue that cost me an afternoon and might save you one.

SAP AI Core distinguishes between two deployment types: `foundation-models` and `orchestration`. LiteLLM's `sap/` model prefix talks exclusively to orchestration deployments it calls `/lm/deployments` and filters for `scenarioId == "orchestration"`. If your deployment is `foundation-models`, the filtered list comes back empty and you get a cryptic `list index out of range` deep inside LiteLLM's transformation module. Not the error message you'd write if you were trying to help someone.

The fix is to switch to the `openai/` prefix with your deployment URL set as `api_base`. LiteLLM then routes through its OpenAI-compatible handler instead, which is perfectly happy talking to either deployment type. It's essentially the airport gate analogy: you got on the right plane, but you were standing at the wrong gate and the gate agent wasn't going to tell you where the plane actually was.

Check your deployment's `scenarioId` in SAP AI Launchpad before choosing your prefix. It's a two-minute check that prevents a two-hour debug session.
 
What It Actually Looks Like
Here is the detailed reference for the setup and what was the thought behind the Architecture design.
https://medium.com/@sabarna17/sap-rpt-and-llm-agents-for-world-cup-2026-football-match-predictions-8ccfa72fa20c
 
 
What This Means Beyond Football
The football scenario is deliberately concrete, but the architecture pattern generalises directly to enterprise use cases:
Maintenance order triage: RPT predicts criticality based on historical order patterns; an LLM agent pulls recent incident notes; synthesis produces a priority score with reasons.
Invoice exception handling: structured approval history as RPT context; free-text comments as LLM enrichment; parallel execution with a timeout on the enrichment path.
Sales forecasting: tabular history through RPT; market news and product launch context through a research model; same 'CONFIDENCE: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW' output pattern.

The common thread is sparse, fast-moving tabular data paired with unstructured context which describes a substantial fraction of what SAP customers actually build on BTP.
 
Final Thoughts
What I didn't expect was how much of the project's value would come from the parts designed to handle failure well: the news timeout, the data quality flags, the explicit `MEDIUM` confidence. In production systems, graceful degradation isn't a nice-to-have. It's what determines whether your users trust the output at all.

If you're working on similar problems tabular prediction on sparse enterprise data, or parallel agent patterns on SAP AI Core I'd love to hear what you're building. Comments are open, and I promise to respond faster than a 120-second timeout.
 
One more thing, the RPT `COLUMNS` list is the contract between the data agent and the model. The first time I added a feature, I had to regenerate the entire context dataset. Treat it like a database migration, not a Python constant.
